# AMBOSS Design System

> A comprehensive design system for AMBOSS products, built with React, TypeScript, and Emotion. Includes components, tokens, utilities, and guidelines for building consistent user interfaces.

This design system provides reusable UI components, design tokens, accessibility guidelines, and testing utilities for AMBOSS applications.
## Introduction

### Accessibility

# Accessibility guidelines

Design system assets such as tokens and components are built with Accessibility in mind, adhering to guidelines from <a href="https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#requirements-for-wcag-2-2" target="_blank">WCAG 2.2</a>. This means a constant attention to color contrast, usage of semantic HTML, WAI ARIA roles and attributes, and keyboard navigation. We regularly test for Accessibility violations with built in <a href="https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-tests/accessibility-testing" target="_blank">Accessibility test addon</a> within each story displayed in Storybook canvas.
This document explains how you can leverage the accessibility API of components to ensure their accessible usage within your application.

<img src="a11y-addon.png" width="100%" alt="A11y addon" />

## Component usage

A lot of accessibility support is internal to DS components. For example, form components are internally associated with hint/error texts with `aria-describedby` attribute. Some components depend on externally provided props to ensure their accessible usage. For example, look out for ARIA label props in each component API. Most of these contain default texts which you can override with your own.

### Text

`Text` component supports text in various semantic colors (primary, success, accent etc). While combining such text with various backgrounds, ensure that the contrast between foreground and background colors meets WCAG 2 AA minimum contrast ratio thresholds.

### Header

`Header` components are available in different styles called `H1` to `H6`. Although, an `H1` component renders an `<h1>` html element by default, you can pass in a different header element to match the header hierarchy on your page. For example,

```html
<H2 as="h1">This is the first heading</H2>
```

### Icon and Image

An `Icon` when used to supplement text should usually be aria-hidden (removed from accessibility tree) and thus not read by screen readers. For example,

```html
<Link ...>External Link <Icon name="external-link" aria-hidden /><Link>
```

An `Icon` or `Image` when used to convey information must provide alternative text content. Since `Icon` component does not render a semantic html element it needs both `img` role and `aria-label`. For example,

```html
<Icon name="alert-triangle" role="img" aria-label="Error" />
<Image source="/amboss-logo.png" alt="AMBOSS logo" />
```
### Button and PictogramButton

`Button` that doesn't contain visible text content, must be provided with an aria-label. For example,

```html
<Button aria-label="Go back to ..."><Icon name="left-arrow" aria-hidden /></Button>
```

If you pass a `label` prop to a `PictogramButton` it will set `aria-label` internally. For example,

```html
<PictogramButton icon="left-arrow" label="Go back to ...">
```

### Card

Card title can be rendered with the desired html header tag using `titleAs` prop, For example,

```html
<Card title="Diabetes Melitus" titleAs="h2" ... />
```

### DropdownMenu

`DropdownMenu` with an icon only trigger must be passed in `triggerAriaLabel` prop. For example,

```html
<DropdownMenu iconName="more-vertical" triggerAriaLabel="Other actions" ... />
```

 ### Form components

 All Form components like Input, Checkbox, Radio etc. are wrapped within an html `label` element. Always pass in a `label` prop value. In case of input elements like Input, SearchInput, etc, if visible label is not desired use the `hideLabel` prop to render a hidden label for accessibility. For example,

 ```html
<Input name="firstName" label="First name" hideLabel ... />
<Checkbox name="selectAllUsers" label="Select all" ... />
 ```

SearchInput also accepts a `label` prop, although it is hidden by default. For example,

```html
<SearchInput label="Search within assigments table" />
```

### FormFieldGroup

Always pass in a label to a `FormFieldGroup`. When there is no visible label, hide it with `isLabelHidden` prop. For example,

```html
<FormFieldGroup label="Select an option" isLabelHidden ... />
```
Also, you can use `labelAs` to render label as one of html heading elements `<h1>` to `<h6>`. For example,

```html
<FormFieldGroup label="Billing address" labelAs="h2" ... />
```

### SegmentedControl

SegmentedControl control is a special case of FormFieldGroup and accepts all its props. Since it is usually used without an visible label, its `isLabelHidden` hidden prop is set to true by default. Make sure to pass a `label` prop. For example,

```html
<SegmentedControl label="Select a plan"  isLabelHidden ... />
```

### Modal

When passed in `header` and `subHeader` prop values, they are used internally within Modal to set `aria-label` and `aria-describedby` attributes within element with `dialog` role.
In case of Modal that does not have a visible `header` or it has only custom content, set `aria-label` value to indicate Modal title. For example,

```html
<Modal header="Delete user?" ... />
<Modal aria-label="Save changes" ... />
```

### Tabs

Set `aria-label` for tab list and a unique id for tab panel. For example,

```html
<Tabs aria-label="Some navigation" tabPanelId="someContainer" ... />
```

### Layout components: Stack and Inline

`Stack` and `Inline` are flex containers primarily used for layout. But sometimes, you may need to render html lists (`ul` or `ol`) using them. For example,

```html
<Stack as="ul">
  <Text> Item 1 </Text>
  <Text> Item 2 </Text>
</Stack>
```


### Theming

## Theming via `Emotion`

Please, import special provider and set of themes that you want to use.
Make sure that ThemeProvider is a most parent component to any use of DS.

Example:

```js

render(
  <ThemeProvider theme={anyCondition ? dark : light}>
    <Routes />
  </ThemeProvider>,
  document.querySelector("#app-container")
);
```

## Theming via `SCSS`

There is `mixins` with css variables. In order to use it add into your project:

```
@use '@amboss/design-system/scss/theming as ds';

@include ds.theming(".your_dark_class_name"); // In order to see result, just add `your_class_name` to html tag.

```

And use themed tokens in your scss files:

```
// in your style/variables/index.scss
@forward "@amboss/design-system/scss/variables";
@forward "my_other_project_variables";

// in your styles.scss
@use '~style/variables/index' as *;

.myComponentClass {
  background-color: $colorBackgroundAccent;

  &:hover {
    background-color: $colorBackgroundAccentHover;
  }
}

```

All tokens are listed in [Colors](?path=/docs/tokens-colors--docs#tokens).

For a full reference of SCSS variables, see [SCSS Variables](?path=/docs/tokens-scss-variables--docs).
For details on the theming mixin API, see [SCSS Theming](?path=/docs/tokens-scss-theming--docs).


## Components

### Avatar

# Avatar

The `Avatar` is commonly used to represent users when a profile image is not available.

## Props

## Usage

The `Avatar` component displays only the first character in uppercase. The avatar has a fixed size of `24x24` pixels.

## Single Avatar

```tsx
<Avatar {...args} />
```

## Profile Menu

```tsx
<InteractiveBox
      variant="tertiary"
      space="xs"
      onClick={() => console.log("clicked")}
    >
      <Inline vAlignItems="center" space="xs" noWrap>
        <Avatar label={label} {...rest} />
        <Stack space="zero">
          <TextClamped size="xs" weight="bold">
            Paula K.
          </TextClamped>
          <TextClamped size="xs" color="tertiary">
            Medical Student
          </TextClamped>
        </Stack>
      </Inline>
    </InteractiveBox>
```

## All examples

```tsx
<Stack space="xxl" alignItems="center">
      <Stack space="m">
        <Inline space="m">
          <Stack space="xs" alignItems="center">
            <Avatar {...args} label="Brand" color="brand" />
            <Text size="xs" color="tertiary">
              Brand
            </Text>
          </Stack>
          <Stack space="xs" alignItems="center">
            <Avatar {...args} label="Gray" color="gray" />
            <Text size="xs" color="tertiary">
              Gray
            </Text>
          </Stack>
          <Stack space="xs" alignItems="center">
            <Avatar {...args} label="Blue" color="blue" />
            <Text size="xs" color="tertiary">
              Blue
            </Text>
          </Stack>
          <Stack space="xs" alignItems="center">
            <Avatar {...args} label="Green" color="green" />
            <Text size="xs" color="tertiary">
              Green
            </Text>
          </Stack>
          <Stack space="xs" alignItems="center">
            <Avatar {...args} label="Yellow" color="yellow" />
            <Text size="xs" color="tertiary">
              Yellow
            </Text>
          </Stack>
          <Stack space="xs" alignItems="center">
            <Avatar {...args} label="Red" color="red" />
            <Text size="xs" color="tertiary">
              Red
            </Text>
          </Stack>
          <Stack space="xs" alignItems="center">
            <Avatar {...args} label="Purple" color="purple" />
            <Text size="xs" color="tertiary">
              Purple
            </Text>
          </Stack>
        </Inline>
      </Stack>

      <Stack space="m">
        <Inline space="m" vAlignItems="center">
          <Stack space="xs" alignItems="center">
            <Avatar {...args} label="Default Tooltip" />
            <Text size="xs" color="tertiary">
              Default Tooltip
            </Text>
          </Stack>
          <Stack space="xs" alignItems="center">
            <Avatar
              {...args}
              label="Custom"
              tooltipContent="Senior Medical Student - Class of 2025"
            />
            <Text size="xs" color="tertiary">
              Custom Tooltip
            </Text>
          </Stack>
          <Stack space="xs" alignItems="center">
            <Avatar {...args} hideTooltip />
            <Text size="xs" color="tertiary">
              No Tooltip
            </Text>
          </Stack>
          <Stack space="xs" alignItems="center">
            <Avatar {...args} icon="ai-sparkle" label="With Icon" />
            <Text size="xs" color="tertiary">
              With Icon
            </Text>
          </Stack>
        </Inline>
      </Stack>
    </Stack>
```

## Accessibility

- When tooltip is enabled, the component is focusable with proper `tabIndex` and focus state for keyboard navigation

## Best Practices

- Don't use `Avatar` for multiple items (use [AvatarGroup](/docs/components-avatargroup--docs) component instead)

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type AvatarProps = {
  /** The name to display in the avatar (first letter will be shown) */
  label: string;
  /** Icon to display instead of the label's initial */
  icon?: IconName;
  color?: AvatarColorProp;
  /** Optional tooltip content. If not provided, will show the full label as tooltip */
  tooltipContent?: string;
  hideTooltip?: boolean;
  /** @deprecated Use aria-* props directly instead */
  ariaAttributes?: AriaAttributes;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
};
```


### AvatarGroup

# AvatarGroup

The `AvatarGroup` component displays a horizontal stack of avatars with overflow counter.

## Props

### Colors

The component cycles through 6 different colors automatically.

```tsx
<AvatarGroup {...args} />
```

## Usage

Use `AvatarGroup` when you need to display multiple users or participants in a compact, visually appealing way. Common use cases include:

- **Contributors**: Show users who have contributed to content
- **Collaborators**: Display people involved in an assignment

## Accessibility

- Each avatar includes proper ARIA attributes for screen readers
- Color is not the only way to distinguish between avatars (letters are also used)
- Proper focus management for keyboard navigation

## Best Practices

- Keep the maximum display of avatars reasonable (2-5 is typically optimal) and use `showCount` to displahy remaining avatars.
- Don't use `AvatarGroup` for single items (use [Avatar](/docs/components-avatar--docs) component instead)

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type AvatarGroupProps = {
  labels: string[];
  /** Maximum number of avatars to display before showing overflow counter */
  maxCount?: number;
  showCount?: boolean;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
};
```


### Badge

# Badge

{/* @missedDoc Write how to add a clickable Badge */}

## All examples

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800 }}>
      <Card>
        <Box space="xxl">
          <Stack>
            <Badge {...args} text="example default" />
            <Badge {...args} text="example green" color="green" />
            <Badge {...args} text="example red" color="red" />
            <Badge {...args} text="example blue" color="blue" />
            <Badge {...args} text="example brand" color="brand" />
            <Badge {...args} text="example purple" color="purple" />
            <Badge {...args} text="example yellow" color="yellow" />
            <Badge {...args} text="example gray" color="gray" />
            <Badge {...args} text="with icon" color="red" icon="bulb" />
          </Stack>
        </Box>
      </Card>
    </div>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type BadgeProps = {
  /** This is a text for a component property */
  text: string;
  /** A color doesn't reflect any intent other than to variety */
  color?: BadgeColor;

  icon?: IconName;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  "data-color"?: BadgeColor;
};
```


### Button

# Button

Buttons are used for interface actions, such as "Add", "Cancel" or "Save".
The `primary` variant should ideally be used only once per view as the main call-to-action,
while other variants can appear more frequently.

For elements that will take users to another page or view upon clicking, use [Link](/story/components-link--docs). See more information about [Button vs Link](#buttons-vs-links).

## Anatomy

1. **Label**
   This is the copy for the button. It typically represents the action that will
   be performed when the button is clicked or tapped. Please refer to [the content
   guidelines](#content-guidelines)
   section for effective call-to-action copy.

2. **Icon left (leading) and right (trailing)**
   Optional icons may be placed before or after label copy. Refer to [the usage
   guidelines](#button-with-icon)
   for information on how and when to use them.

3. **Target area**
   The base clickable or tappable area of the button.

## Props

## Variants

### Primary

Use a `primary` button to draw attention to the most important call-to-action.
As a general rule, primary buttons should only appear once per view.
However, not every screen requires a primary button.
The most important thing is to establish a visual hierarchy between the buttons in your UI.

### Secondary

Use a `secondary` button to complement `primary` button with related actions, such as “Cancel".

### Tertiary

`Tertiary` button is subtle in its appearance
as it does not have a visible shape encompassing the label.
Therefore, it can be used for supplementary actions or for
a group of multiple actions on a given UI.

## Examples

### Single button

```tsx
<Button {...args}>Click me</Button>
```

### Button sizes

Buttons come in three sizes: default (M), small and large.
As a rule of thumb, we recommend using the default size for buttons
unless there is a good reason to display a larger or smaller button.

When designing a UI, it's common to display multiple actions on the same page.
In addition to using different button variants and placements, you can also use sizes
to create a visual hierarchy that helps users focus on the most important task.

### Button with icon

Icons should be used intentionally and not just as decorative elements.
In some cases, they can serve as supplementary elements to draw attention
to important actions and help users identify the action quickly.
This works well when an icon has a "universal" meaning associated with it.
For instance, an arrow pointing to the left indicates navigating backward
(in the left-to-right reading direction).

```tsx
<div style={{ width: "100%", maxWidth: "560px" }}>
      <Inline alignItems="spaceBetween">
        <Button {...args} variant="tertiary" leftIcon="arrow-left">
          Back
        </Button>
        <Inline space="s">
          <Button {...args} variant="secondary" leftIcon="bookmark">
            Bookmark
          </Button>
          <Button {...args} leftIcon="download">
            Download
          </Button>
        </Inline>
      </Inline>
    </div>
```

### Destructive buttons

When indicating the criticality of an action that is irreversible,
such as deleting or closing an account, use a destructive button
in combination with other visual cues. These buttons are typically
found in confirmation modals that appear before the action is completed.

```tsx
<StatefulDestructiveModal {...args} viewMode={viewMode} />
```

### Full-width button

Buttons can be expanded to full width to fill their parent containers by adding
the `fullWidth` prop. This is particularly useful on pages like a dashboard where
it's important to display consistent button widths in multiple containers that
are visible alongside each other. Doing so reduces visual clutter.

```tsx
<div style={{ maxWidth: 680 }}>
      <Columns gap="l">
        <OneButtonCard
          title="Latest Qbank sessions"
          iconName="quiz"
          boldText="Comprehensive question bank"
          subText="with 100% coverage of USMLE® & NBME® exam content"
          buttonArgs={{
            fullWidth: true,
            variant: "primary",
            children: "Start your first Qbank session",
          }}
        />

        <OneButtonCard
          title="Recently viewed articles"
          iconName="article"
          boldText="Exclusive Library features"
          subText="designed to amplify your study sessions and skill"
          buttonArgs={{
            fullWidth: true,
            variant: "secondary",
            children: "Go to Library",
          }}
        />
      </Columns>
    </div>
```

### Button as a link

In a few exceptional cases, when directing the user to another page or view
is one of the primary tasks, a button as a link can be used instead of a regular
[hyperlink](/story/components-link--docs).
This provides the necessary visual hierarchy to draw the user's attention to the link.

The Button component can be used as link by specifying the `href` prop.

```tsx
<Button {...args}>Go to google</Button>
```

## States

Buttons come with hover, clicked (or pressed) and focus states.
Additionally, two properties are available for use when they are relevant to user flows.

### Loading state

The button component can display a loading state by specifying a `loading` prop.
Check out the [All buttons story](/story/components-button--all-buttons) to see how it looks.

### Disabled state

Use this state for actions that are currently unavailable.
The surrounding interface should make it clear why the button
is disabled and what needs to be done to enable it.
Check out the [All buttons story](/story/components-button--all-buttons) to see how it looks.

## Best practices / When to use

- Prioritize the most important actions by guiding users to focus on what they need to do next, with only one main call-to-action in any given view or section. Too many calls to action can cause confusion.
- Use established button color variants appropriately. For example, only use a red destructive button for an action that is difficult or impossible to undo.
- Position buttons consistently throughout the interface, especially in a series of tasks that make up a single user flow.

## Content guidelines

- Actions should be concise, clear and lead to predictable outcomes. As a rule of thumb, the character count should not exceed 30 characters.
- Begin with a strong, actionable verb. The following core structure and conventions govern actions:
  > **Action verb + term (+ differentiator or context if required)**
- CTA copy is always written in sentence case.
- For scannability and brevity, avoid adding articles such as "a," "an," or "the," unless the article omission creates ambiguity.
- CTA copy is written without punctuation (no periods, exclamation marks, question marks, commas, etc.).

## Accessibility

### Aria label

Use Aria label for a button in the following cases:

- When the visible label of the button does not adequately convey its purpose to non-visual users.
- When the button has no text and relies on an icon alone to convey its purpose. Check out the Pictogram Story.

## Related component

- To combine or lay out multiple buttons, use [ButtonGroup](/story/patterns-buttongroup--docs).
- For navigational actions that appear within or directly following a sentence, use [Link](/story/components-link--docs).

### Buttons vs Links

Buttons are primarily used for actions, such as "Add", "Close", "Cancel", or "Save".
Links, on the other hand, are primarily used for navigation and usually appear within
or directly following a sentence or paragraph of text.

In short, a button _performs an action_, while a link _takes the user to a destination_.
However, this simple rule may not be appropriate for every case.
Use [Button as a link](/story/components-button--button-as-link) to draw the user's attention to a link, if necessary.

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
type ButtonPrivateProps = {
  squareCorners?:
    | boolean
    | ("TopLeft" | "TopRight" | "BottomLeft" | "BottomRight")[];
  alignItems?: HorizontalAlignment;
  /* @deprecated: Use rightIconColor prop instead */
  rightIconVariant?: TextVariations;
  rightIconColor?: IconsColors;
};

export type ButtonProps = {
  variant?: BaseVariations;
  size?: ButtonSize | MQ<ButtonSize>;
  /** Type is ignored if "a" is provided to "as" property. Note that you can explicitly pass null. */
  type?: "submit" | "button" | "reset" | null;
  disabled?: boolean;
  /** When loading is true, disabled is set to true as well. */
  loading?: boolean;
  onClick?: (e: React.MouseEvent) => void;
  onFocus?: (e: React.FocusEvent) => void;
  onBlur?: (e: React.FocusEvent) => void;
  onPointerDown?: (e: React.PointerEvent) => void;
  leftIcon?: IconName;
  rightIcon?: IconName;
  fullWidth?: boolean;
  destructive?: boolean;
  privateProps?: ButtonPrivateProps;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  /** @deprecated Use aria-* props directly instead */
  ariaAttributes?: AriaAttributes;
};
```


### Callout

# Callout

Callouts are used to contextually highlight important information within the content of a page. They are persistent, non-dismissible elements that remain visible to ensure users have access to essential information. Unlike feedback mechanisms, callouts do not disappear and are always present on the screen.

## Anatomy

1. **Icon**
   Each functional variant comes with a default icon, which can be customized as needed.
2. **Callout Text**
   The primary message. Keep it concise, especially when a description is included.
3. **Description (optional)**
   Provides additional context or detailed information to complement the callout text. Use it for longer explanations.

## Props

## General properties & examples

### Functional variants

Callouts are available in five functional color variants. Choose the appropriate variant to ensure a cohesive user experience:

- **Success**: Use to communicate positive messages.
- **Error**: Use to convey critical or negative messages that require attention.
- **Warning**: Use to highlight important cautionary information or potential issues.
- **Info**: Use to share neutral, informational messages that provide context or additional details.
- **Neutral**: Use to visually highlight short, non-critical information without implying urgency or severity.

### Basic callout

```tsx
<div style={{ width: "80%" }}>
      <Callout text={text} description={description} icon={icon} type={type} />
    </div>
```

### Callout with link

```tsx
<div style={{ width: "80%" }}>
      <Callout text={text} description={description} icon={icon} type={type} />
    </div>
```

### Callout with link as button

```tsx
<div style={{ width: "80%" }}>
      <Callout text={text} description={description} icon={icon} type={type} />
    </div>
```

### Callout with description

```tsx
<div style={{ width: "80%" }}>
      <Callout text={text} description={description} icon={icon} type={type} />
    </div>
```

### All functional variants

```tsx
<div style={{ width: "80%" }}>
      <Stack space="xs">
        <Callout text="Info" />
        <Callout
          text={
            <>
              Success message with{" "}
              <Link as="button" onClick={() => alert("clickety click")}>
                a link
              </Link>{" "}
              with a click handler.
            </>
          }
          type="success"
        />
        <Callout
          text="Warning"
          description={
            <>
              Some text with <Link href="/url">a link</Link> pointing to a URL.
            </>
          }
          type="warning"
        />
        <Callout
          text="Error. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Nam atorpa turpis viverra sem. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit st amet."
          description="This is an optional description. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Nam atorpa turpis viverra sem. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit st amet."
          type="error"
        />
        <Callout
          text="Neutral message"
          description="This is an optional description."
          type="neutral"
        />
        <Callout
          text="AI message"
          description={
            <>
              Ask any clinical question like{" "}
              <Link href="/url">
                “What drugs are FDA approved for children with depression?”
              </Link>
              .
            </>
          }
          type="ai"
        />
      </Stack>
    </div>
```

## Best practices / When to use

- Use callouts to draw attention to critical information that supplements the main content.
- Ensure callout content is brief and scannable. The title should clearly convey the purpose or importance of the message.
- Avoid adding multiple callouts to a topic. This dilutes the importance of the callout and distracts the user.
- Avoid creating callouts with irrelevant content or using them purely for decoration, as this can lead to users ignoring future callouts.

<DialogDecisionTable />

<Notifications />

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type CalloutProps = {
  type?: CalloutVariations;
  icon?: IconName;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  text: string | ReactElement;
  description?: string | ReactElement;
};
```


### Card

# Card

Cards are used to group related information together in a visually similar elevated surface. Use cards to organize content into digestible sections that users can easily scan.

## Anatomy

1. **Title**
   Optional header text that appears at the top of the card. The HTML element used for the title can be customized via the `titleAs` prop (defaults to `div`), allowing for proper semantic structure when needed (e.g., using heading elements like `h1`, `h2`, etc.).

2. **Dropdown menu**
   Optional dropdown menu that appears in the card header, typically used for contextual actions related to the card's content.

3. **Content area**
   The content area does not include padding by default. Use the `CardBox` component to add consistent and responsive padding.

## Props

## Card preview

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, height: 250 }}>
      <Card {...rest}>{children}</Card>
    </div>
```

## Simple card

> No header
> note that the content of a card doesn't have a padding.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, height: 250 }}>
      <Card {...rest}>{children}</Card>
    </div>
```

## Add Padding to a Card

Box components are used to provide a padding and some alignment to content.
For Card content, we created `<CardBox />` component to achieve that.
You can also add Different actions to a Card header

### Card with box content and dropdown action in header

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, height: 250 }}>
      <Card {...rest}>{children}</Card>
    </div>
```

### Card with link action in header

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, height: 250 }}>
      <Card {...rest}>{children}</Card>
    </div>
```

## Related components

- <a href="/?path=/docs/components-catalogcard--docs" target="_blank">
    CatalogCard
  </a>
- <a href="/?path=/docs/components-container--docs" target="_blank">
    Container
  </a>

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type CardProps = {
  /** Specify title displayed in Header */
  title?: string;
  /** Specify what the html element of of the card header should be */
  titleAs?: HeaderProps["as"];
  action?: CardAction;
  overflow?: "auto" | "hidden" | "visible";
  squareCorners?: boolean;
  outlined?: boolean;
  id?: string;
  children?: React.ReactNode;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
};
```


### CatalogCard

# CatalogCard

CatalogCard is a small, interactive card, with title and optional description.
The CatalogCard supports a flexible topLeftElement.
A DropdownMenu can be shown in the top right corner.

## Basic Catalog Card

## With Dropdown Menu

## With topLeftElement

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
// Shared types for component props
type TopRightComponentProps = {
  portalContainer?: PortalProps["portalContainer"];
};

type TopRightDropdownMenuProps = TopRightComponentProps & {
  menuItems: DropdownItem[];
  onMenuOpen?: (ev: MouseEvent | KeyboardEvent) => void;
  "aria-label"?: string;
};

type TopRightColorPickerProps = TopRightComponentProps & {
  colorPickerProps: ColorPickerProps;
  menuItems: (DropdownItem | ColorPickerDropdownItemProps)[];
};

export type CatalogCardProps = Omit<
  ContainerProps,
  "id" | "elevation" | "overflow" | "squareCorners" | "children"
> & {
  title: string;
  subtitle?: string;
  subtitleLines?: number | "none";
  dropdownMenuOptions?: (DropdownItem | ColorPickerDropdownItemProps)[];
  colorPickerProps?: ColorPickerProps;
  portalContainer?: PortalProps["portalContainer"];
  onClick?: VoidFunction;
  onDropdownClick?: (ev: MouseEvent | KeyboardEvent) => void;
  topLeftElement?: React.ReactNode;
  slotProps?: {
    dropdownMenu?: {
      "aria-label"?: string;
    };
    title?: {
      as?: "h2" | "h3" | "h4" | "h5" | "h6" | "span" | "p";
    };
  };
};
```


### Collapsible

# Collapsible

{/* @missedDoc describe CollapsibleHeader as subcomponent and put in Meta */}
Collapsible is used to put long sections of information under a block that be can expanded or collapsed.
It allows to hide and reveal sections of content underneath the associated header.

- **Do** use`<CollapsibleHeader/>` as the first element of children
- **Do** add any number of collapsible items.
- **Do** use `<Divider />` to separate multiple `<Collapsible />` and create an accordion.

## Props

## Collapsible example usage

In this example, we are using Collapsible to toggle the visibility of content by clicking content's header.

## Collapsible with icon in the header

## Collapsible with long content

## Collapsible expanded on default

## Multiple Collapsible separated by `<Divider />`

In this example, we are using multiple Collapsible to toggle the visibility of three different contents.

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type CollapsibleProps = {
  /** the first element of the array of children has to be  `<CollapsibleHeader/>`. The rest of the children is the content visible after spreading the component */
  children: [
    React.ReactElement<CollapsibleHeaderProps>,
    ...React.ReactElement[]
  ];
  isExpanded: boolean | undefined;
  overflow?: Property.Overflow;
  reduceMotion?: boolean;
};

export type CollapsibleHeaderProps = {
  onClick?: (e: React.FormEvent<HTMLButtonElement>) => void;
  expandedIconAriaLabel?: string;
  collapsedIconAriaLabel?: string;
} & React.ComponentProps<typeof Box>;
```


### ColorIndicator

# ColorIndicator

A visual indicator component for displaying color swatches, currently used for chart color contexts. Supports different `shapes`, `sizes`, and optional children.

## Props

## Basic Usage

<Canvas of={ColorIndicatorStories.Basic}>
  <Story id="components-colorindicator--basic" />
</Canvas>

## All Variants

<Canvas of={ColorIndicatorStories.AllVariants}>
  <Story id="components-colorindicator--all-variants" />
</Canvas>

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type ColorIndicatorProps = {
  color:
    | "gray"
    | "blue"
    | "green"
    | "yellow"
    | "red"
    | "purple"
    | "neonBlue"
    | "neonGreen"
    | "neonPink";
} & Omit<ColorDotProps, "hexCode"> &
```


### ColorPicker

# ColorPicker

A component for selecting a color from a predefined palette. Used for categorization of Collections, in personal highlighting, and similar. Supports different `colors`, `sizes`, and a `disabled` state (for use when loading). Selection is managed via props.

## Props

## Basic Usage

<Canvas of={ColorPickerStories.Basic}>
  <Story id="next-composite-colorpicker--basic" />
</Canvas>

## Disabled State

<Canvas of={ColorPickerStories.Disabled}>
  <Story id="next-composite-colorpicker--disabled" />
</Canvas>

## With Selection

<Canvas of={ColorPickerStories.WithSelection}>
  <Story id="next-composite-colorpicker--with-selection" />
</Canvas>

## Accessibility

- Each color option is rendered as a button, ensuring keyboard accessibility and screen reader support.
- The selected color is visually indicated and announced via the button's state.
- If using icons only, provide an `aria-label` or similar accessible name for the button if the color meaning is not visually clear.
- Follows accessibility best practices from the underlying [Button](/?path=/docs/components-button--docs) component.

## Related components

- <a href="/?path=/docs/next-composite-colorindicator--docs" target="_blank">
    ColorIndicator
  </a>
- <a href="/?path=/docs/components-button--docs" target="_blank">
    Button
  </a>

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type ColorPickerProps = Pick<ColorPickerOptionProps, "size"> & {
  alignItems?: InlineProps["alignItems"];
  colors: Array<ColorOption>;
  disabled?: boolean;
  onColorSelected: (color: ColorPickerOptionProps["color"]) => void;
  selectedColor?: ColorPickerOptionProps["color"];
  /** @deprecated Use aria-* props directly instead */
  ariaAttributes?: AriaAttributes;
};
```


### Container

# Container

A container is an element used to visually hold any content and UI elements together. Unlike cards, which are used to display a set of objects, containers offer more flexibility and can be utilized in a wide range of scenarios. For [a detailed comparison between Containers and Cards](#containers-vs-cards), refer to the last section of this document.

## Props

## Container preview

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800 }}>
      <Container {...args}>
        <Box space="xl">
          <Text>Example text</Text>
        </Box>
      </Container>
    </div>
```

## Elevation and border radius

### Elevation

A container is used to construct the foundational surface for other components, and different elevation levels can be applied to create visual hierarchy. Shadows are used to create the perception of various depths, expressing a model of layered surfaces that forms the structure of the UI.

As the elevation levels increase from 1 to 4, the span of shadow and blur increases along the X-axis and Y-axis. The higher an object is along the Z-axis, the elevation level increases. Apply elevations thoughtfully, recognizing that users can relate to real-world behavior and will naturally focus more on objects that appear closer to the eye. Changes in elevations can also be used to express interaction, such as hover or drag states of objects.

Below is a list of a few examples of components that utilize containers at different elevation levels.

{/* prettier-ignore */}
<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th> Elevation </th>
      <th> Component </th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>1</td>
      <td>
        <a href="/?path=/docs/components-card--docs" target="_blank">Card</a>, <a href="/?path=/docs/components-datatable--docs" target="_blank">Data table</a>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>2</td>
      <td>Top bar</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>3</td>
      <td>
        <a href="/?path=/docs/components-dropdownmenu--docs" target="_blank">Dropdown menu</a>, <a href="/?path=/docs/components-popover--docs" target="_blank">Popover</a>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>4</td>
      <td>
        <a href="/?path=/docs/patterns-modal--docs" target="_blank">Modal</a>
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Border radius

Similar to elevation, border radius can be applied to containers to mimic real-world behavior, thereby reducing cognitive load for users interacting with the interface. Surfaces attached to the edge of the screens should have no rounded corners. Conversely, floating objects can have rounded corners, and the closer objects appear to the eye, the rounder the corners can be.

## Best practices & considerations

- In a view where containers are displayed next to each other, such as a dashboard, consider maintaining uniform height for both the container and its content. This will create a well-balanced interface.
- When a container is used to host a large amount of content, consider using dividers to separate pieces of information. This helps pace the content and enables users to focus on one piece at a time.

## Related components

### Containers vs Cards

Containers and Cards share several visual qualities, resulting in a similar appearance. However, distinctions exist between them, guiding when to use each to ensure visual and interactive clarity in the UI.

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th> Container </th>
      <th> Card* </th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <th>Visualization</th>
      <td>
        <ul>
          <li>
            A container is a visual element (a box) that hold any content or
            elements.
          </li>
          <li>
            Can be presented in isolation or alongside other containers. The
            dimensions can be varied.
          </li>
          <li>Difficult to interchange with other forms of UI structure.</li>
        </ul>
      </td>
      <td>
        <ul>
          <li>
            A card is an object presented in a form that resembles a physical
            card.
          </li>
          <li>
            Usually presented as a group of the same type of object, having
            identical dimension.
          </li>
          <li>
            It is possible to interchange a set of cards with another UI
            structure, i.e. a list.
          </li>
        </ul>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <th>Content</th>
      <td>
        <ul>
          <li>
            A large set of information and actions can be displayed within one
            container.
          </li>
          <li>
            The content structure can be unique within a particular container.
          </li>
          <li>A container can contain cards.</li>
        </ul>
      </td>
      <td>
        <ul>
          <li>
            A focus, condensed information about one particular topic is
            displayed.
          </li>
          <li>
            The content structure is consistent and comparable to other cards
            displayed alongside it.
          </li>
          <li>A card can't contain a container.</li>
        </ul>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <th>Interaction</th>
      <td>
        <ul>
          <li>
            The primary actions are with the content and not with the container
            itself.
          </li>
        </ul>
      </td>
      <td>
        <ul>
          <li>
            The card (=the object) is directly interactive. The user can click
            or tap the whole card to access the detail view.
          </li>
          <li>
            There might be optional actions (=shortcuts) that allow user to
            directly manage the object, e.g. save, delete, etc.
          </li>
        </ul>
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

- <i>
    This is a conceptual definition that we aspire to achieve. The current
    design system's Card component does not align with these criteria and will
    be adjusted accordingly.
  </i>

## Containers on mobile UI

To maximize the use of limited horizontal real estate on smaller mobile devices and visually distinguish containers from directly interactive cards, display containers full-width without any margins on the mobile breakpoint and on mobile native app UIs at the smallest size class.

<img src="containers-on-mobile.png" alt="Containers on mobile UI" />

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
type ContainerPrivateProps = {
  border: boolean;
};

export type ContainerProps = {
  elevation?: 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4;
  borderRadius?: BorderRadius | MQ<BorderRadius>;
  overflow?: "auto" | "hidden" | "visible";
  /** When true, renders a light border. Independent of elevation; both can be used together. */
  outlined?: boolean;
  children?: React.ReactNode;
  squareCorners?: boolean;
  id?: string;
  // TODO border is gonna be in main props
  privateProps?: ContainerPrivateProps;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  /** @ignore */
  "data-ds-id"?: string;
};
```


### CountButton

# CountButton

CountButton is a button component that displays a count badge next to the button label.
It uses the secondary button style and is ideal for actions that need to show a numerical value.

## Props

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type CountButtonProps = {
  /** Count to show in tag. If null, tag does not show. */
  count: number | null;
} & Omit<ButtonProps, "variant" | "destructive">;
```


### DataTable

# Data Table

Data tables are used to efficiently organize and display information from a data set. It provides insight into sets of information, as it helps users compare, order, find and manage the data easily.
_Not to be confused with "[Regular table](#related-component)," which is a layout mechanism for structuring content._

## Anatomy

1. **Header row**
   This row contains titles for the columns, along with optional sorting behavior. The data column used to sort the displayed data should be visible, and the sorting order should be indicated accordingly.

2. **Table rows**
   This document contains table cells that can host different types of data. Rows come in three different densities: Compact (S), Default (M), and Comfortable (L).

3. **Footer**
   This optional element hosts the pagination component, which allows users to browse through a large data set as pages when the data cannot be shown all at once.

## Props

## Variants

### Basic data table

By default, the table is rendered inside of a Container component, with elevation 1, and has an 'auto' layout, which means that the columns are sized according to their content. However, a minimum width can be set on the table container. When the viewport is resized, the table can be scrolled horizontally if it overflows the container.

The first column of the data table can be set as "sticky" via the `isFirstColumnSticky` prop. When this option is set to "true" (default), the column will always be visible while the rest of the table is scrolled underneath it. This option is useful when the data table is large, as it provides the user with better orientation while browsing and reading its content.

There is also an option to set the last column of the table to be sticky with the `isLastColumnSticky` prop, which is particularly useful when actions are presented on the table rows. There is `useDataTableSort` hook for helping do sort.

```tsx
<DataTableWithScrollableContainer {...args} />
```

### Predefined column widths

Alternatively, the table width can be specified and the column width can be set by percentage or pixel value. Similar to the default table, the minimum width of the container can be set. This means that when the data table is resized, the cells will adjust according to your specifications.

> Note: In `fixed` table layout, columns are sized according to the table width and the remainiing space is distributed between columns. Thus, column width acts as the minimum width.

```tsx
<div style={{ minWidth: "10rem" }}>
      <DataTable {...args} />
    </div>
```

### Data table with footer

This can be used when the dataset is large and needs to be split into multiple pages. The footer contains the [pagination](?path=/docs/components-pagination--docs) component, which enables the user to navigate through the entire dataset.

```tsx
<div style={{ minWidth: "10rem" }}>
      <DataTable {...args} />
    </div>
```

### Scrollable body with sticky header row

In certain cases, the dataset might need to be presented as a flat list without pagination. The table provides an option to set a height, either as an absolute number or as a percentage size relative to the view or parent. When the content exceeds the available space, the body becomes scrollable, while the header row remains sticky at the top of the data table.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: "735px" }}>
      <DataTable {...args} />
    </div>
```

### Without container

Use `BaseDataTable` component to render the table without `Container` wrapper.

```tsx
<div style={{ minWidth: "10rem" }}>
      <BaseDataTable {...args} />
    </div>
```

### With Toolbar

Use `BaseDataTable` and `BulkActionsToolbar` components and wrap it into `Container`. There is `useDataTableQueryFilter` hook for helping do search filter.
`Actions` and `renderRightContent` props of the `BulkActionsToolbar` allow you to insert various call-to-action elements.

```tsx
<TableWithToolbar {...args} />
```

### With selectable rows

```tsx
<TableWithSelectableRows {...args} />
```

### With toggletips

```tsx
<div
      style={{
        minWidth: "10rem",
      }}
    >
      <DataTableWithToggletips {...args} />
    </div>
```

## Best practices / When to use

- Do not use this component as a layout mechanism to present content that cannot be sorted, reordered, or managed by the user. Please refer to the "[Data Table vs. Regular Table](#related-component)" of this document.
- Avoid placing data tables inside modals or smaller containers where the information can feel cramped or require truncation.
- The hover state for data table rows should always be enabled as it can help users visually scan the columns of data in a row, even if the row is not interactive.
- Display interactive content and elements clearly when the user interacts with a table row.
- Columns can be sorted in ascending or descending order. Sortable columns should be clearly indicated with an icon next to the column title. A sortable column has three states: unsorted (`arrows`), sorted-up (`arrow--up`), or sorted-down (`arrow--down`). The icon indicates the current sorted state.

## States

### Loading state

The loading state displays the data table header row with a loading indicator below it. The content of the header cells informs users about the information that can be expected when the dataset is loaded.

If the dataset is not too large, we recommend loading the entire set to enable a smooth browsing experience when users navigate between pages of the data table.

### Empty and error state

If the data set is not yet available to be displayed, consider adding a message that informs the user about the system state or the user action that would affect the creation of that data set.

```tsx
<DataTable {...args}>
      <Stack space="xxs">
        <H4>Unable to load the data</H4>
        <div>
          <Text color="tertiary" size="s" align="center">
            We can&apos;t seem to be able to connect to server. Please try
            again.
          </Text>
          <Text color="tertiary" size="s" align="center">
            If the problem persists, contact customer support.
          </Text>
        </div>
      </Stack>
    </DataTable>
```

## Content guideline

- Column header titles should be concise yet descriptive of the data in that column.
- Wrap the content instead of truncating it. This is because if row titles start with the same word, they will all appear the same when truncated.
- Alignment of content:
  - Numerical = Right aligned
  - Textual data = Left aligned
  - Align headers with their related data
  - Don’t center align content

## Accessibility

- Data table is rendered with HTML `<table>` markup.
- It accepts a `caption` prop that is used to display hidden caption for screen readers.
- Currently sorted column has an `aria-sort` attribute with sort direction.

## Related component

### Data table vs Regular table

<table width="80%">
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td></td>
      <td>
        <b>Data table</b>
      </td>
      <td>
        <b>Regular table</b>
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td></td>
      <td>
        Used to display a set of similar “objects” with several properties,
        allowing the users to compare, find and manage these objects easily.
      </td>
      <td>
        Used as a layout method to structure content, allowing the users to read
        and compare relationship of information of the same topic easily.
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Sortable</td>
      <td>✔️</td>
      <td>Optional / Not by default</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Filterable</td>
      <td>✔️</td>
      <td>Optional / not by default</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>“Dynamic” content</td>
      <td>✔️</td>
      <td>✖️</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>A row has interactions / actions</td>
      <td>✔️</td>
      <td>✖️</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Has pagination</td>
      <td>✔️</td>
      <td>✖️</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>——</td>
      <td>——</td>
      <td>——</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Cell content</td>
      <td>Varied, depends on “data”</td>
      <td>Text or images, could contain hyperlinks</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Visual quality</td>
      <td>
        In the same column, each cell presents the properties in a visually
        uniform way
      </td>
      <td>
        Each cell in the same column could have varying lengths of text or types
        of content
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Mergeable cells</td>
      <td>✖️</td>
      <td>✔️</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Truncation of content</td>
      <td>✔️</td>
      <td>✖️</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Can be presented as a list or a grid of cards?</td>
      <td>✔️</td>
      <td>Not easily / needs rewriting</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type DataTableProps = {
  /** Screen reader text for table caption */
  caption: string;

  /** CSS table layout. In 'auto' layout, columns are sized according to content width. In 'fixed' layout, columns can be provided a fixed or percentage width. */
  layout?: "auto" | "fixed";

  /** Table width */
  width?: string | MQ<string>;

  /** Vertical padding on row cells to set table density, extra extra small (xxs), extra small (xs), condensed (s), default (m), comfortable (l) */
  bodyCellVerticalPadding?:
    | "xxs"
    | "xs"
    | "s"
    | "m"
    | "l"
    | MQ<"xxs" | "xs" | "s" | "m" | "l">;

  /** Height of empty/error or loading table content */
  emptyTableContentHeight?: string | MQ<string>;

  /** Footer element */
  footer?: React.ReactElement;

  /**
   * Meta data for columns
   * @param DataTableColumn[].name - Column name
   * @param DataTableColumn[].label - Column label for display
   * @param DataTableColumn[].align - Align cell content, 'left' | 'right' | 'center'
   * @param DataTableColumn[].width - Column width
   * @param DataTableColumn[].toggletipContent - Content of right toggletip
   * @param DataTableColumn[].renderCell - Callback to render custom cell content.
   * @param DataTableColumn[].isSortable - Is table sortable by column
   * @param DataTableColumn[].sortDirection - Sorted as 'asc' | 'desc'
   */
  columns: DataTableColumn[];

  /**
   * Table content as an array of objects with values for each column
   * @param DataTableRow[].id - Unique id
   * @param DataTableRow[].isSelectable - Whether row can be selected
   */
  rows?: DataTableRow[];

  /**
   * Is loading data. You can display custom loading content with children prop.
   */
  isLoading?: boolean;

  /** Screen reader text for loading spinner */
  loadingStateScreenReaderText?: string;

  /** Is empty or in error state. You can display custom empty/error content with children prop.
   */
  isEmpty?: boolean;

  /**
   * Is first column sticky on horizontal scroll
   */
  isFirstColumnSticky?: boolean;

  /**
   * Is first column sticky on horizontal scroll
   */
  isLastColumnSticky?: boolean;

  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;

  /**  Column most recently used to sort data */
  currentlySortedByColumn?: string;

  /**
   * Empty cell content.
   */
  emptyCellContent?: string;

  /** Define a vertically scrollable table with max height */
  maxHeight?: string | MQ<string>;

  /** Renders selectable rows */
  isSelectable?: boolean;

  /** Array of selected row ids */
  selectedRowIds?: DataTableRow["id"][];

  /** Indicates that all rows are selected */
  allRowsSelected?: boolean;

  selectAllRowsCheckboxAriaLabel?: string;

  /** Callback to handle selection change */
  onRowSelectionChange?: (selectedRowIds: DataTableRow["id"][]) => void;

  /** Callback to handle all rows selection change */
  onAllRowsSelectionChange?: (allRowsSelected: boolean) => void;

  /**
   * Callback to handle sorting by column
   */
  onSort?: (
    columnName: string,
    desiredSortDirection: DataTableColumn["sortDirection"]
  ) => void;

  /** Customize row  */
  getRowProps?: (row: DataTableRow) => DataTableRowProps;
};

type TableContainerProps = Pick<DataTableProps, "footer" | "maxHeight">;

const TableContainer = styled.div<TableContainerProps>(
  ({ theme, footer, maxHeight }) => ({
    position: "relative",
    zIndex: 0, // create a stacking context for sticky headers
    overflow: "auto",
    backgroundColor: theme.values.color.background.primary.default,
    borderRadius: "inherit",

    ...useResponsiveValue({
      maxHeight,
    }),

    ...(footer && {
      borderBottomLeftRadius: 0,
      borderBottomRightRadius: 0,
    }),
  })

type StyledTableProps = Pick<DataTableProps, "layout"> & {
  tableWidth: DataTableProps["width"];
};
```


### Divider

# Divider

Divider is used as a visual separator between parts of content. It can be used horizontally to separate content vertically, or vertically to separate content horizontally.

- **Do not** use to surround an item, use a border.
- **Do not** use after `<CardHeader />` component. CardHeader uses Divider by default
- **Do** use to separate items in a card or any content area.
- **Do** use vertical dividers to separate inline elements like navigation items or tabs.

## Props

## Basic Divider

In this example, we are using Divider to separate different sections in a card component.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800 }}>
      <Card>
        <Stack space="zero">
          <CardBox>
            <Text weight="bold">section 1</Text>
          </CardBox>
          <Divider text={text} />
          <CardBox>
            <Text weight="bold">section 2</Text>
          </CardBox>
          <Divider />
          <CardBox>
            <Text weight="bold">section 3</Text>
          </CardBox>
        </Stack>
      </Card>
    </div>
```

## Divider with text

You can add descriptive text to a divider to provide context about the separation.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800 }}>
      <Card>
        <Stack space="zero">
          <CardBox>
            <Text weight="bold">section 1</Text>
          </CardBox>
          <Divider text={text} />
          <CardBox>
            <Text weight="bold">section 2</Text>
          </CardBox>
          <Divider />
          <CardBox>
            <Text weight="bold">section 3</Text>
          </CardBox>
        </Stack>
      </Card>
    </div>
```

## Vertical Divider

Use vertical dividers to separate inline content like navigation items, tabs, or breadcrumbs. The vertical divider supports different sizes and automatically adapts to the height of its container.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 300 }}>
      <Card>
        <CardBox>
          <Inline space="m" vAlignItems="center" alignItems="center">
            <PictogramButton icon="storefront" label="Shop" />
            <Divider orientation={orientation} size={size} />
            <PictogramButton icon="ai-chat" label="Lisa" />
            <PictogramButton icon="lists" label="Collections" />
            <PictogramButton icon="help-circle" label="Help & Privacy" />
            <PictogramButton icon="user" label="Settings" />
          </Inline>
        </CardBox>
      </Card>
    </div>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type DividerProps = {
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  /** To be used only when orientation is horizontal. */
  text?: string;
  orientation?: "horizontal" | "vertical";
  /**
   * To be used only when orientation is vertical.
   * Represents the height of the vertical Divider.
   * @default 'm'
   */
  size?: Sizes;
};
```


### DropdownMenu

# DropdownMenu

A dropdown menu is a compact way of displaying multiple choices. It appears upon interaction with a button.
It can be used if you want to show user that there're more actions other than the main CTA to choose from.
Dropdown menu uses `<MenuItem/>` component which is not exported for public use.

Behaviour:

- Interaction with the button opens the menu.
- Menu closes when it's not on focus.
- Selecting an menu item closes the menu.
- Menu is positioned below the button and right aligned by default. If there's not enough space below it is positioned on top of the button.

Keyboard shortcut:

- Open menu: enter || space || up || down.
- Close menu: escape || enter || space.
- Navigate menu items: up || down.
- Select menu item: enter || space.

## Props

### Basic usage

- An array of menu item with its own label and onSelect must be provided.
- `overflow="unset` is needed if you are using dropdown menu in a card.

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 150 }}>
      <DropdownMenu {...props} />
    </div>
```

### With label

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 150 }}>
      <DropdownMenu {...props} />
    </div>
```

### With different control icon

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 150 }}>
      <DropdownMenu {...props} />
    </div>
```

### With separator

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 150 }}>
      <DropdownMenu {...props} />
    </div>
```

### With menu item icon

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 150 }}>
      <DropdownMenu {...props} />
    </div>
```

### With menu text variant none

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 150 }}>
      <DropdownMenu {...props} />
    </div>
```

### With emphasized menu item

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 150 }}>
      <DropdownMenu {...props} />
    </div>
```

### With destructive menu item

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 150 }}>
      <DropdownMenu {...props} />
    </div>
```

### With disabled menu item

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 150 }}>
      <DropdownMenu {...props} />
    </div>
```

### With disabled control button

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 150 }}>
      <DropdownMenu {...props} />
    </div>
```

### With open and close menu callback

```tsx
<OpenCloseCallbackTemplate {...props} />
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type DropdownPrivateProps = {
  dropdownSquareCorners?:
    | boolean
    | ("TopLeft" | "TopRight" | "BottomLeft" | "BottomRight")[];
};

export type DropdownMenuProps = {
  /** an array of menu items, contains "label" and "onSelect" */
  menuItems: DropdownItem[];
  /** the text variant of menu items */
  menuItemTextVariant?: "uppercase" | "capitalize" | "lowercase" | "none";
  /* Icon name */
  iconName?: IconName;
  /* Dropdown button disabled state */
  disabled?: boolean;
  /* aria-label for trigger (especially if it only has icon) */
  triggerAriaLabel?: string;
  /** Dropdown menu open state callback */
  onMenuOpen?: (e: MouseEvent | KeyboardEvent) => void;
  /** Dropdown menu close state callback */
  onMenuClose?: (e: MouseEvent | KeyboardEvent) => void;
  privateProps?: DropdownPrivateProps;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
} & Pick<PortalProps, "portalContainer"> &

type ConditionalProps =
  | { size?: "xs"; label?: never }
```


### EntityList

# EntityList

An EntityList vertically groups a series of similar or related data entries and can contain both read-only and interactive information. For hierarchical data entries, use <a href="/?path=/docs/next-composite-entitytree--docs" target="_blank">EntityTree</a>. To display data entries with multiple parameters and allow the user to sort the data, use <a href="/?path=/docs/components-datatable--docs" target="_blank">DataTable</a>.

## Anatomy

1. **Label**
   The only required element of a list item is a `label`. Long labels will be wrapped into multiple lines by default.
2. **Leading icon**
   A list item can include a leading icon as a supplemental element.
3. **Description**
   An optional description can be used to display secondary information.
4. **Trailing element**
   Customize the component by adding additional trailing UI elements or secondary actions to the list item. Use `renderRightContent` prop.
5. **Divider**
   Dividers are visible by default, with an option to hide them.
6. **Checkbox**
   In the selectable variant, checkboxes are displayed. Clicking on the entire item will select it.
7. **Item density**
   Entity list items come in four different densities: Extra compact (XS), Compact (S), Default (M), and Comfortable (L).

## Props

## Variants

### Basic entity list

In its most basic form, an entity list consists of a single continuous column of rows, with each row representing a list item. The only required element is the label of an item.

List items are provided in four different densities. Select the appropriate one based on the complexity of your content and other elements within the user interface.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 600 }}>
      <Stack space="m">
        <EntityList
          {...args}
          size="m"
          renderRightContent={({ badge }) =>
            badge ? <Badge color="green" text={badge} /> : undefined
          }
        />
        <EntityList {...args} size="s" />
        <EntityList {...args} size="xs" />
      </Stack>
    </div>
```

### With active state and other properties

Leading icons can be displayed as visual anchor points to aid user orientation. However, use them purposefully and not decoratively.

An additional UI element can be placed at the end of an item when needed. When possible, try to keep the height consistent across items to ensure good scannability.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800 }}>
      <Columns gap="l">
        <Column size={[12, 6]}>
          <EntityList
            {...args}
            role="menu"
            getLeftIconProps={args.getLeftIconProps}
            data={mainActiveListData}
            renderRightContent={({ actionSize, isActive }) =>
              isActive ? (
                <Icon
                  size={actionSize}
                  color="quaternary"
                  name="chevron-right"
                />
              ) : null
            }
          />
        </Column>
        <Column>
          <EntityList
            {...args}
            onClick={args.onClick}
            getLeftIconProps={args.getLeftIconProps}
            data={extraActiveListData}
            renderRightContent={({ actionSize, id, tabIndex }) =>
              id === "1" || id === "2" ? (
                <Inline>
                  <PictogramButton
                    icon="chevron-up"
                    size={actionSize}
                    aria-label="Favorite"
                    tabIndex={tabIndex}
                    onClick={() => console.log("click button")}
                  />
                  <PictogramButton
                    icon="chevron-down"
                    size={actionSize}
                    aria-label="Favorite"
                    tabIndex={tabIndex}
                    onClick={() => console.log("click button")}
                  />
                </Inline>
              ) : null
            }
          />
        </Column>
      </Columns>
    </div>
```

### Selectable entity list

A selectable entity list allows the user to select multiple data entries and perform bulk actions on them. The primary action of a selectable entity list is selecting; clicking on the entire row will select the item. This is different from a simple group of checkboxes which is used to present independent, binary choices.

The bulk action toolbar, which includes search by keyword functionality, can be appended to the entity list.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 600 }}>
      <StatefulList {...args} />
    </div>
```

## Behaviours

### Layout behaviour

- An entity list does include a default `Container`. If you don't need it then import `BaseEntityList`.
- On mobile breakpoint, the horizontal padding of each item is reduced. Scale down your browser width to observe the responsive behaviour.

### Interaction

- A read-only entity list does not have states.
- For an interactive item, the hover state provides a visual cue to the user that the item is interactive.
- The primary action of an item is activated when the entire item is clicked.

### Virtual list

Virtual lists are optimized for rendering large datasets efficiently by only rendering visible items in the viewport. This approach significantly improves performance when dealing with hundreds or thousands of list items.

Virtual lists work best with infinite scroll patterns for exploration, while paginated approaches provide better control for targeted item searches.

#### When to use virtual lists

- **Large datasets**: When rendering more than 100-200 items that could impact performance
- **Browsing and exploration**: When users need to scroll through extensive data to discover and explore available content
- **Unknown dataset size**: When the total number of items is unknown or dynamically loaded
- **Memory optimization**: When you need to minimize DOM nodes and memory usage

To enable the virtual list behavior, pass the isVirtualized and maxHeight props to the EntityList component.

## Best practices / When to use

- Use entity list to present data entries logically. Sort items in a way that is easy for users to understand, such as alphabetically or chronologically.
- Keep the information concise and easy to scan.
- Display icons, text, and actions in a consistent format. Avoid adding decorative elements.
- When adding a custom element, ensure the padding around the element is adjusted to align it vertically with the title text and maintain consistent row height, creating the right visual balance.
- Do not mix different types or densities of items within one list to maintain scannability. Scannability is improved when elements are placed in consistent locations across list items.
- Do not use selectable entity list to present a group of independent choices. Use a <a href="/?path=/story/form-checkbox--checkbox-group" target="_blank">checkbox group</a> instead.

## Related components

- <a href="/?path=/docs/next-composite-entitytree--docs" target="_blank">
    EntityTree
  </a>
- <a href="/?path=/docs/components-datatable--docs" target="_blank">
    DataTable
  </a>


### EntityTree

# EntityTree

An EntityTree displays a series of basic hierarchical data and allows users to browse and manage them. To display data entries with multiple parameters and enable sorting, use <a href="/?path=/docs/components-datatable--docs" target="_blank">DataTable</a>.

## Anatomy

1. **Label**
   The only required element of a tree item is a label. Long labels will be wrapped into multiple lines by default.
2. **Children count**
   If an item is a branch, the optional [count of children] can be shown. In the selectable variant, this number will be [selected / total count of children].
3. **Expand and collapse toggle**
   The chevron direction indicates the state of each branch and also acts as a button to expand or collapse each branch independently. By default, every branch is collapsed. However, in certain cases where users should be directed to a specific branch or item, the tree can be configured to open with a specific branch already expanded.
4. **Description**
   An optional description can be used to display secondary information.
5. **Trailing element**
   Customize the component by adding additional trailing UI elements or secondary actions to the list item.
6. **Divider**
   Dividers are visible by default, with an option to hide them.
7. **Checkbox**
   In the selectable variant, checkboxes are displayed. Clicking on the entire item will select it.
8. **Item density**
   Entity list items come in four different densities: Extra compact (**XS**), Compact (**S**), Default (**M**), and Comfortable (**L**).

## Props

## Variants

### Basic entity tree

Use a basic entity tree to present a set of hierarchical data, allowing users to navigate through nested levels of information. The visual indentation of each level conveys parent-child relationships, making it easy to understand the structure and dependencies within the data set.

By default, the entity tree presents the data set in an all-collapsed state. Clicking on the entire row of a branch will expand or collapse it. Secondary actions or other custom elements can be added as needed.

```tsx
<Stack space="xl">
      <BasicStatefulTree />
      <BasicStatefulTree size="s" />
      <BasicStatefulTree size="xs" />
    </Stack>
```

### Selectable entity tree

A selectable entity tree allows the user to select multiple data entries and perform bulk actions on them. The primary action of a selectable entity tree is selecting; clicking on the entire row will select the item. This is different from a simple group of checkboxes which is used to present independent, binary choices.

```tsx
<Container overflow="hidden">
      <SelectedStatefulTree {...args} />
    </Container>
```

### With bulk action tool bar (Searchable tree)

The bulk action toolbar, which includes search by keyword functionality, can be appended to the entity tree. Typing in a search term will show items with titles that contain matched characters, keeping the branch(es) visible for context. The checked state of hidden items will be retained.

When filtered, the expand and collapse toggle becomes disabled.

**Tech notes:**

- The search functionality is provided through the `filterFn` prop.
- Use `getBulkActionToolbarCheckboxProps` helper function from `BulkActionsToolbar/getCheckboxProps` to get the checkbox props for the bulk action toolbar. Wrap this function in useMemo if you have a big tree data.

```tsx
<Container overflow="hidden">
      <SelectedStatefulTreeWithActionToolber {...args} />
    </Container>
```

## Behaviours

### Layout behaviour

- An entity tree does not include a default container. Place it inside a <a href="/?path=/docs/components-container--docs" target="_blank">container</a> to visually contain it and elevate it from the background.
- The entity tree doesn’t include a label for the “Show more” button due to localization. This button appears when the number of nodes exceeds the value defined in the `chunkSize` prop, which defaults to 50. A client should pass it through the `showMoreButtonLabel` prop.
- On mobile breakpoints, the horizontal padding of each item is reduced. Additionally, the indentation size of each item's lower-level branch is decreased. Scale down your browser width to observe the responsive behavior.

### Interaction

- The primary action of an item is activated when the entire item is clicked.
  - For the basic entity tree, clicking on the entire row of a branch will expand or collapse it.
  - For the selectable variant, clicking on the entire row will select or deselect the item. If an item is a branch, all children of that branch will be selected or deselected accordingly.
- An entity tree is navigable by arrow keys. Pressing `enter` will activate the primary action of the item. Disabled items will be skipped.

## Best practices / When to use

- Use entity tree to present a basic hierarchical data series. To display data entries with multiple parameters and enable sorting by those parameters, use <a href="/?path=/docs/components-datatable--docs" target="_blank">DataTable</a>.
- Keep the information concise and easy to scan.
- Display icons, text, and actions in a consistent format. Avoid adding decorative elements.
- When adding a custom element, ensure the padding around the element is adjusted to align it vertically with the title text and maintain consistent row height, creating the right visual balance.
- Do not mix different types or densities of items within one list to maintain scannability. Scannability is improved when elements are placed in consistent locations across list items.
- Do not use selectable entity list to present a group of independent choices. Use a <a href="/?path=/story/form-checkbox--checkbox-group" target="_blank">checkbox group</a> instead.

## Accessibility

WIP

- An entity tree is navigable by arrow keys. Pressing `enter` will activate the primary action of the item. Disabled items will be skipped.
- When adding additional elements, ensure they are aria-labeled according to the guidelines specific to those components.

## Related components

- <a href="/?path=/docs/components-entitylist--docs" target="_blank">
    EntityList
  </a>
- <a href="/?path=/docs/components-datatable--docs" target="_blank">
    DataTable
  </a>


### Icon

# Icon

## Props

## Basic Icon

```tsx
<Text as="span">
      <Icon {...props} inline />
    </Text>
```

## Hoverable Icon

- **Do** use hoverable icon when your icon is clickable
- **Do not** use boolean hoverable type if your icon is nested in a parent, and you want the hover state to match the parent. Check the next example for that use case.

```tsx
<Text as="span">
      <Icon {...props} inline />
    </Text>
```

## Hoverable Icon with hoverable parent

- **Do** use this if you want your icon hover state to match the parent

### Demo

In the example below, our Hoverable Parent is a Button. The icon is expected to change opacity when the button is hovered.

```tsx
<NestedIconComponent size={size} />
```

### Code example

<Source
  language="tsx"
  dark
  code={`

export const YourFancyComponent = () => {
  const ref = useRef();
  return (
    <HoverableParent ref={ref}>
      <Icon size="s" name="article" hoverable={ref} />
    </HoverableParent>
  )
};
`}
/>

## Inline Icon

- **Do** use this if you want to use Icon inline, e.g. with text or links

```tsx
<Link href="amboss.com" color="tertiary">
      External link
      <Icon {...props} />
    </Link>
```

## All colors of icon

```tsx
<Inline vAlignItems="center">
      <Icon name={name} color="primary" size={size} />
      <Icon name={name} color="secondary" size={size} />
      <Icon name={name} color="tertiary" size={size} />
      <Icon name={name} color="quaternary" size={size} />
      <Icon name={name} color="brand" size={size} />
      <Icon name={name} color="accent" size={size} />
      <Icon name={name} color="error" size={size} />
      <Icon name={name} color="info" size={size} />
      <Icon name={name} color="warning" size={size} />
      <DemoAccentContainer>
        <Box space="xxs">
          <Icon name={name} color="onAccent" size={size} />
        </Box>
      </DemoAccentContainer>
    </Inline>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type IconProps = {
  /** Icon name */
  name: IconName;
  /** set to true to make the icon hoverable, pass a parent's ref to make the icon match the parent hover */
  hoverable?: boolean | React.MutableRefObject<any>;
  /** set to true to have an inline icon wrapped in a span instead of div */
  inline?: boolean;
  /** @deprecated variant, please use "color" prop instead */
  variant?: IconsVariations;
  /** Icon color can inherit parent style. */
  color?: IconsColors;
  size?: IconSizes | MQ<IconSizes>;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  "data-testid"?: string;
} & Omit<HTMLAttributes<HTMLDivElement>, "color">;
```


### IconTile

# IconTile

The `IconTile` component is a circular container for icons, with a border and icon color that can be customized.

## Props

## Colors

The `color` prop can be used to change the color of the icon and border.

```tsx
<Card>
      <Box space="xxl" alignText="center">
        <Inline space="m">
          <IconTile icon="star" />
          <IconTile icon="star" color="brand" />
          <IconTile icon="star" color="blue" />
          <IconTile icon="star" color="yellow" />
          <IconTile icon="star" color="green" />
          <IconTile icon="star" color="red" />
          <IconTile icon="star" color="purple" />
          <IconTile icon="star" color="gray" />
        </Inline>
      </Box>
    </Card>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type IconTileProps = {
  icon: IconName;
  color?: IconTileColor;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  "data-color"?: IconTileColor;
};
```


### Image

# Image

This component renders an HTML [picture](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/picture) element and accepts all props of an HTML [img](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLImageElement/Image) element.

## Basic

## With fallback src

Provide a fallback src when original image fails to load.

## With placeholder

Provide a placeholder/loading image src to display while original image is loading

```tsx
<Stack>
      <Text>
        Test with throttled network to notice placeholder image while original
        image loads
      </Text>
      <Image {...args} />
    </Stack>
```

## With multiple sources

Provide HTML [source](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/source) element attributes or [img](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLImageElement/Image) `srcset` attribute to define multiple sources for different screen sizes, densities etc.

```tsx
<Stack>
      <Text>
        Re-size viewport to view a different image on smaller breakpoint
      </Text>
      <Image {...args} />
    </Stack>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type ImageProps = ImgHTMLAttributes<HTMLImageElement> & {
  /** src for a placeholder image to be shown while loading the original image */
  placeholderSrc?: string;
  /** src for a fallback image if original image cannot be loaded */
  fallbackSrc?: string;
  /** provide props for source elements to define multiple srcsets */
  sources?: SourceHTMLAttributes<HTMLSourceElement>[];
  /** lazy load image */
  lazy?: boolean;
  /** maps to object-fit css */
  fit?: Property.ObjectFit | MQ<Property.ObjectFit>;
  /** maps to object-position css */
  position?: Property.ObjectPosition | MQ<Property.ObjectPosition>;
  borderRadius?: BorderRadius | MQ<BorderRadius>;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
};

type StyledImgProps = Pick<ImageProps, "fit" | "position" | "borderRadius">;

const StyledImg = styled.img<StyledImgProps>(
  ({ theme, borderRadius }) =>
```


### Link

# Link
