Links are used as navigational elements, directing users to another page or view. They may appear on their own as standalone elements, or within a sentence or paragraph as inline links.

For actions, such as “Add”, “Cancel”, or “Save”, use <a href="/?path=/docs/components-button--docs" target="_blank">Button</a>. See more information about [Links vs Buttons](#links-vs-buttons).

## Anatomy

1. **Text**
   This is the visible copy of the link. It should clearly and concisely convey where the link goes or what action it performs. Please refer to [the content guidelines](#content-guidelines) for best practices on writing effective link copy.

2. **Icon left (leading) and right (trailing)**
   You can optionally include an icon before or after the link text to provide additional context or improve scannability.

3. **Target area**
   This defines the clickable/tappable region of the link.

## Props

Also, inherits all properties of HTML `a` and `button` elements.

## Usage and visual styles

### Standalone vs Inline

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 600 }}>
      <Columns vAlignItems="center" alignItems="center">
        <StyledColumn size={[12, 6, 6]}>
          <StyledBox>
            <Link size={size} {...rest}>
              Standalone link
            </Link>
          </StyledBox>
        </StyledColumn>
        <Column size={[12, 6, 6]}>
          <StyledBox>
            <Text as="span" size={size}>
              This is an <Link {...rest}>inline link</Link>.
            </Text>
          </StyledBox>
        </Column>
      </Columns>
    </div>
```

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Usage</th>
      <th>Visual styles</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <th>Standalone</th>
      <td>
        When a link is used on its own or directly following a paragraph of
        content, it will not be underlined. By default, the link will appear in
        the accent color. A standalone link can be paired with an icon when
        appropriate.
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <th>Inline</th>
      <td>
        When links are used within a sentence or paragraph, they will be styled
        with an underline. By default, the link adopts the text style of the
        surrounding paragraph, but it can be overridden.
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

### Standalone

When a link is used on its own or directly following a paragraph of content, it will not be underlined. By default, the link will appear in the accent color. A standalone link can be paired with an icon when appropriate.

When placing a link after a paragraph, the size of the link should match the size of that paragraph of text.

### Inline

When links are used within a sentence or paragraph, they will be styled with an underline. By default, the link adopts the text style of the surrounding paragraph, but it can be overridden.

```tsx
<Card>
      <CardBox>
        <Stack>
          <Text>
            Cognitive restructuring: The patient identifies{" "}
            <Link href="/">negative automatic thoughts</Link> (i.e.,
            dysfunctional/negative view of the self, future, or world) in order
            to dispute them and eventually replace them with healthy{" "}
            <Link href="/">thought processes</Link>.
          </Text>
          <Text color="tertiary">
            Epidemiological data refers to the US, unless otherwise{" "}
            <Link href="/">specified</Link>.
          </Text>
          <Text>
            Reciprocal translocation between chromosome 9 and chromosome 22 →
            formation of the{" "}
            <Link weight="bold" href="/">
              Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22)
            </Link>{" "}
            → fusion of the ABL1 gene (chromosome 9) with the BCR gene
            (chromosome 22) → formation of the{" "}
            <Link weight="bold" href="/">
              BCR-ABL gene
            </Link>{" "}
            → encodes a BCR-ABL non-receptor tyrosine kinase with increased
            enzyme activity
          </Text>
          <Text color="secondary">
            Additional <Link href="/">chromosomal</Link> changes and mutations
            of <Link href="/">tumor suppressor genes</Link> and oncogenes (p53,
            Rb1, or Ras), which emerge during the course of the disease, are
            responsible for the progression from chronic to accelerated phase
            and, ultimately, the transition to acute leukemia.
          </Text>
          <Link href="/">Read more</Link>

          <Callout type="info" text={calloutText()} />
          <Callout type="success" text={calloutText()} />
        </Stack>
      </CardBox>
    </Card>
```

### Link as a button

In exceptional cases, you may need to use link to trigger actions in addition to navigation.

```tsx
// eslint-disable-next-line no-alert
    <Link as="button" onClick={() => alert("clickety click")} {...props}>
      Link as button
    </Link>
```

### All links

```tsx
<Stack space="xl">
      <Stack>
        <Link href="/">
          A standalone link which inherited the size from its parent.
        </Link>
        <Link href="/" size="xs">
          A standalone link, size xs
        </Link>
        <Link href="/" size="s">
          A standalone link, size s
        </Link>
        <Link href="/" size="m" color="primary">
          A standalone link, size m in text.primary color.
        </Link>
        <Link href="/" iconLeft={{ name: "external-link" }} size="s">
          Small link with icon
        </Link>
        <Link
          href="/"
          inline={false}
          iconRight={{ name: "external-link" }}
          size="m"
          color="secondary"
        >
          Medium link with icon
        </Link>
        <Link href="/" iconRight={{ name: "chevron-right", color: "tertiary" }}>
          Link with different icon color
        </Link>
      </Stack>
      <Stack>
        <Text size="m">
          This is <Link href="/">a link</Link> inside a size-m text.
        </Text>
        <Text>
          This is{" "}
          <Link href="/" weight="bold">
            a bolded link
          </Link>{" "}
          inside a text.
        </Text>
        <Text>
          This is{" "}
          <Link href="/" weight="bold" color="accent">
            a bolded and accented link
          </Link>{" "}
          inside a text.
        </Text>
        <Text weight="bold">
          This is <Link href="/">a link</Link> inside a bolded text.
        </Text>
        <Text weight="bold" color="secondary">
          This is <Link href="/">a link</Link> inside a bolded secondary text.
        </Text>
        <Text weight="bold" color="tertiary">
          This is <Link href="/">a link</Link> inside a bolded tertiary text.
        </Text>
        <H1>
          This is{" "}
          <Link href="/" inline color="primary">
            a link
          </Link>{" "}
          inside an H1.
        </H1>
        <H1 color="tertiary">
          This is{" "}
          <Link href="/" inline color="tertiary">
            a link
          </Link>{" "}
          inside a tertiary H1.
        </H1>
      </Stack>
    </Stack>
```

## Content guidelines

Since links redirect users to a different location, the text used for the link should be descriptive enough to indicate the destination without relying on the surrounding text. It is best to avoid using terms like "click here" or the web address itself.

## Accessibility

If a link is used to perform an action or if there is an `onClick` event, it should be used with `as="button"` and vice versa.

## Related component

### Links vs Buttons

Links are primarily used for navigation and usually appear within or directly following a sentence or paragraph of text. Buttons, on the other hand, are primarily used for actions, such as "Add", "Close", "Cancel", or "Save".

In short, a button _performs an action_, while a link _takes the user to a destination_.

## Pro tip

If you want to use this Link with your router of choice, you can use property `as`.

> Note that you can provide any props you need, for example: to, that is required by React Router.

<Source
  language="tsx"
  dark
  code={` // your application
  import { Link } from "@amboss/design-system";
  import { Link as RouterLink } from "react-router-dom";
  export const MyFancyComponent = () => {
    return (
      <div>
        some important content
        <Link as={RouterLink} to="/"> Go to home </Link>
      </div>
    )
  };
`}
/>

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type LinkConditionalProps =
  | {
      /** It forces to inherit color, weight, size from parent */
      inline: true;
      /** A color is inherited from its parent Text otherwise set as 'primary' */
      color: TextColors;
      /** Icons are not supported for inline links */
      iconLeft?: never;
      /** Icons are not supported for inline links */
      iconRight?: never;
    }

export type LinkProps = {
  /** A size is inherited from its parent */
  size?: TextSize;
  /** A weight is inherited from its parent */
  weight?: TextWeight;
  /** @deprecated variant, please use "color" prop instead */
  variant?: LinkVariations;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
} & LinkConditionalProps &
```


### MediaItem

# MediaItem

MediaItem can be used as a preview of any kind of media.
The media size can be set with pre-defined sizes or custom values. By default it fills out the width of the container.

## Props

## Examples

### Basic Media Item

### Sizes

```tsx
<Inline space="m" vAlignItems="bottom">
      {(["xs", "s", "m", "l", "xl"] as MediaItemSize[]).map((size) => (
        <MediaItem key={size} {...args} size={size} />
      ))}
    </Inline>
```

### Custom Size

```tsx
<MediaItem {...args} />
```

### Fill container width

```tsx
<div style={{ maxWidth: "400px" }}>
      <Columns gap="xl">
        <Column size={6}>
          <Stack space="m">
            <Text align="center">Square</Text>
            <MediaItem {...args} squareByWidth />
          </Stack>
        </Column>
        <Column size={6}>
          <Stack space="m">
            <Text align="center">Fixed height</Text>
            <MediaItem {...args} height="100px" />
          </Stack>
        </Column>
      </Columns>
    </div>
```

### Src error

```tsx
<MediaItem {...args} />
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type MediaItemProps = {
  src: string;
  title: string;
  /** Display or hide the title underneath the media. */
  showTitle?: boolean;
  /** Pre-defined media sizes. */
  size?: MediaItemSize;
  /** Sets the width of the media if the size is not set. */
  width?: Property.Width;
  /** Sets the height of the media if the size is not set. */
  height?: Property.Height;
  /** Sets the height of the media to equal the width to make it a square. */
  squareByWidth?: boolean;
  /** Sets the object-fit value of the image. */
  fit?: "cover" | "contain";
  categoryLabel?: string;
  categoryIcon?: IconName;
  /** Sets the placement of the category relative to the image.<br/>
   * "inside": only the category icon is required<br/>
   * "outside": both category icon and label are required */
  categoryPlacement?: "inside" | "outside";
  /** Sets the background color behind the media that is visible if the fit is "contain". */
  backgroundColor?: "white" | "black" | "transparent";
  /** Sets the hyphens of the title text. */
  hyphens?: Hyphens;
  /** Sets the active state of the media item. */
  isActive?: boolean;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
};
```


### Notification

# Notification

Notification banners are high-priority, persistent messages displayed prominently at the top of the page to communicate system-wide announcements, critical errors, or important updates that affect the entire application. Unlike toasts, notifications remain visible until the user takes action or explicitly dismisses them, making them ideal for information that requires user awareness or acknowledgment.

## Anatomy

1. **Icon**
   Each functional variant comes with a default icon, which can be customized as needed. Set to `null` to hide icon.
2. **Notification Text**
   The primary message. Use `expandable` to render additional content.
3. **Call to action**
4. **Dismiss button (optional)**

## Props

## Notification example usage

In this example, we are using a Notification to display a confirmation of a successful action.

```tsx
<Stack space="xs">
      <Notification {...args} />
      <Notification type="success" {...args} />
      <Notification type="error" {...args} />
    </Stack>
```

## With Link

Notifications can include links within the text content for additional context or actions.

## Without Icon

Notifications can be displayed without an icon when a more minimal appearance is desired.

## With Dismiss Button

Notifications can include a dismiss button to allow users to close them manually.

## With Call To Action

Notifications can include a call-to-action button for primary actions related to the notification.

## With Expandable Content

Notifications can include expandable content for additional details or information.

## Long Text

Notifications handle longer text content gracefully while maintaining readability.

## Long Text With Call To Action

Notifications with longer text can also include call-to-action buttons.

## Decision Framework

Use this framework to choose the most appropriate notification component for your use case. Each component serves different purposes and contexts.

## Best practices / When to use

### Appropriate use cases

- System-wide status changes (maintenance mode, new features)
- Critical errors that affect the entire application
- Important announcements that require user awareness
- Persistent reminders for incomplete required actions

<DialogDecisionTable />

### Content guidelines

- **Be concise but complete**: Provide enough context for users to understand the situation
- **Include clear next steps**: If action is required, make it obvious what the user should do
- **Use appropriate urgency**: Match the tone to the actual importance
- **Provide dismissal clarity**: If dismissible, make it clear this won't show again

### Timing and behavior

- Show notifications at the top of the page load when relevant
- Don't show multiple notifications simultaneously unless absolutely necessary
- Consider auto-dismissal for non-critical informational messages
- Persist error notifications until the issue is resolved or explicitly dismissed

## Content guidelines

### Message structure

- **Primary text**: Clear, specific description of the situation
- **Call-to-action**: Action-oriented button text ("Update now", "Review settings")
- **Expandable content**: Use for additional details, troubleshooting steps, or related information

### Writing tone

- **Error notifications**: Helpful and solution-focused, not accusatory
- **Success notifications**: Celebratory but brief
- **Info notifications**: Clear and informative without being overwhelming

<Notifications />

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type NotificationProps = {
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  type?: Extract<SubThemeName, "info" | "success" | "error">;
  text?: string | ReactElement;
  icon?: IconName | null;
  isDismissable?: boolean;
  callToActionLabel?: string;
  expandButtonLabel?: string;
  shrinkButtonLabel?: string;
  expandable?: boolean;
  /** This prop introduces additional space to the left to account for the side navigation collapse button in ui-amboss */
  adjustForSideNavToggle?: boolean;
  closeButtonAriaLabel?: string;
  onClickCallToAction?: (e: React.MouseEvent) => void;
  onClickDismiss?: () => void;
  children?: React.ReactNode;
};
```


### Pagination

# Pagination

Pagination lets users browse a collection of items that has been split into several pages. It provides them with a control for navigating to the next or previous page.

## Anatomy

### Numbered pagination (Default)

Numbered pagination shows page number buttons for direct navigation to any page. It includes Previous/Next buttons with text labels and displays page numbers with ellipsis for large page counts.

1. **Previous and Next buttons with labels** - Enable navigation with increased hit area
2. **Page number buttons** - Allow direct navigation to specific pages
3. **Ellipsis** - Indicate truncated page numbers for large collections
4. **Active page** - Visually distinct with accent color

**Mobile variant**: On mobile viewports, page numbers are replaced with a compact "X/Y" format.

### Compact pagination

Compact pagination shows only Previous/Next buttons with a label indicating the current position.

![PaginationAnatomy.png](pagination-anatomy.png)

1. **Previous and next buttons** - Enable the user to navigate to the next or previous page. Buttons are disabled on first/last page.
2. **Label** - Provides information about location within the collection (e.g., "1 of 10" or "1-10 of 100")

## Props

## Best practices / When to use

### Choosing a variant

- **Numbered pagination (default)**: Use when users benefit from jumping to specific pages (e.g., search results, long lists with known structure). Provides direct page access and better overview of total pages.
- **Compact pagination**: Use for simple sequential navigation or when space is limited. Best for linear browsing where users typically navigate one page at a time.

### General guidelines

- Only segment content into pages when it makes browsing easier.
- Place pagination at the end of the page content.
- **For numbered pagination**: Consider the total page count. The component works best with 5-20 pages. For very large page counts (>100), consider additional filtering or search options.
- **For compact pagination with item ranges**: Avoid showing too few or too many items per page. We generally recommend displaying at least 10 items per page and no more than 20-25 items per page. This recommendation may vary depending on the visual density chosen.
  - Displaying too many items per page may be overwhelming
  - Displaying too few items per page may be frustrating to browse

## Examples

### Numbered Pagination

#### Default

```tsx
<PaginationWithState {...args} />
```

#### Icon Only Buttons

Navigation buttons without text labels for more compact display.

```tsx
<NumberedPaginationWithState {...args} />
```

### Compact Pagination

#### Basic

Basic pagination displays the current page number.

```tsx
<CompactPaginationWithState totalItems={args.totalItems} />
```

#### With Item Range

Displays the range of items currently visible (e.g., "1-10 of 100").

```tsx
<CompactPaginationWithState {...args} />
```

#### Custom Label

```tsx
<CompactPaginationWithState
      {...args}
      formatLabel={(
        firstItemIndex?: number,
        lastItemIndex?: number
      ): string => {
        const total = args.totalItems;
        if (firstItemIndex === lastItemIndex) {
          return `Question ${firstItemIndex} of ${total}`;
        }
        return `Question ${firstItemIndex} - ${lastItemIndex} of ${total}`;
      }}
    />
  ),
};
```

#### Label Alignment

The compact variant supports two label alignment options via the `alignLabel` prop:

**Center-aligned (default)**: The label is centered between the prev/next buttons. We recommend positioning the component in the center of the content view.

![pagination-default-layout.png](pagination-default-layout.png)

**Left-aligned**: When pagination is positioned at the bottom right of the content view, use `alignLabel="left"`. The next and previous buttons are displayed next to each other, with the label to the left of them.

```tsx
<CompactPaginationWithState
      {...args}
      formatLabel={(
        firstItemIndex?: number,
        lastItemIndex?: number
      ): string => {
        const total = args.totalItems;
        if (firstItemIndex === lastItemIndex) {
          return `Question ${firstItemIndex} of ${total}`;
        }
        return `Question ${firstItemIndex} - ${lastItemIndex} of ${total}`;
      }}
    />
  ),
};
```

![pagination-left-aligned-layout.png](pagination-left-aligned-layout.png)


### PictogramButton

# Pictogram Button

A pictogram button is a button that uses an icon instead of text to convey an action. This is particularly useful in interfaces where space is limited, such as toolbars, dashboards, or data tables. Use pictogram buttons sparingly and preferably for common actions that users are likely to recognize immediately, such as previous/next, search, settings, or add.

## Props

## General properties

- Pictogram buttons are available in three different sizes and three different color variants that correspond to the regular <a href="/?path=/docs/components-button--docs" target="_blank">button component</a>
- When a label is provided, a tooltip will be displayed on hover. This is highly recommended as it enhances the clarity and usability of the interface.

## Basic Pictogram Button

### Pictogram button without label

## Best Practices / When to use

- Display tooltips on hover to enhance clarity and usability of the interface. This feature is provided out-of-the-box if a label is supplied when the button is used.
- Avoid ambiguous or unfamiliar icons. Ensure the icon clearly represents the action it triggers. For example, use an arrow for navigating to the next step, a pencil for editing, or a plus sign for adding new items.
- Use icons consistently across the application to avoid confusing users.

## Accessibility

Provide ARIA labels to ensure that screen readers can describe the button's function.

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type PictogramButtonProps = {
  /** The centered icon to be displayed. */
  icon: IconName;
  /** If true, the button has square corners. */
  squareCorners?: boolean;
  /** Size */
  size?: "xs" | "s" | "m";
  variant?: PictogramVariations;
  label?: string;
} & Omit<
```


### Popover

# Popover

A Popover is a floating non-modal UI element that opens on demand. It can be used to provide users with additional extra content.

## General properties

- Popovers can contain text and interactive elements, such as links or buttons.
- Popovers are displayed when users click on a trigger or when they keyboard-focus on it and hit enter.
- Popovers remain visible until the trigger is clicked again, there is an outside click, or the Escape key is pressed.
- To ensure that the Popover is fully visible, it is position-aware. By default, it uses [auto-placement](?path=/story/components-Popover--auto-placements). If there is not enough space on the viewport to display the Popover in its default placement, it will automatically use alternative pointer positions and/or placements instead.
- The default placement is on the bottom of the UI element that triggers it.
- The default pointer position is at the center of the Popover.
- There is no padding added to the container and it will need to be added in the children.
- Unless differently specified, the maximum width for the popovers is 400 px.

## Props

## Usage

```javascript

// Popover, use case 1: Wrap trigger element
const App = () => (
  <Popover content={<div>Some text with links/buttons</div>}>
    <Link href="#Popover">text</Link>
  </Popover>
);

// Popover, use case 2: Provide external trigger ref
const App = () => (
  <Popover
    content={<div>Some text with links/buttons</div>}
    externalTriggerRef={triggerRef}
  />
);
```

## Popover with auto placement

```tsx
<PopoverWithTrigger {...args} />
```

## Popover with no arrow showing

```tsx
<PopoverWithTrigger {...args} />
```

## Popover with specific placement

In certain situations where the default placement of a Popover is not desired because it may obscure important information, you have the option to manually specify where the Popover should appear.

```tsx
<PopoverWithTrigger {...args} />
```

## Best Practices / When to use

- Use a Popover to present additional information that doesn’t need to be shown immediately (e.g. phrasionaries in articles).
- Use Popovers instead of <a href="/?path=/docs/components-tooltip--docs" target="_blank">tooltips</a> when the content contains interactive elements, such as links or buttons, to make the component accessible.
- It's important to consider the context in which the Popover appears. The content of the Popover should be relevant to the specific elements on the page.

<OverlayDecisionTable />

## Accessibility

- The Popover is triggered by a click event.
- The trigger always has `aria-expanded` set to `true` or `false` depending on the Popover visibility. When open, `aria-controls` is also added pointing to the Popover content.
- When the Popover is opened, focus is transferred to the first interactive element within the Popover. Focus remains trapped inside the Popover until it is dismissed.
- The Popover can be closed by clicking the trigger, clicking outside of it, or pressing the Escape key.

## Related components

{/* prettier-ignore */}
- <a href="/?path=/docs/components-toggletip--docs" target="_blank">Toggletip</a>
- <a href="/?path=/docs/components-tooltip--docs" target="_blank">Tooltip</a>
- <a href="/?path=/docs/patterns-modal--docs" target="_blank">Modal</a>

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type PopoverProps = Omit<
  BasePopoverProps,
  "contentPadding" | "name" | "subTheme"
> &
  TooltipConditionalProps;

export function Popover({
  renderAsSheetOnMobile = true,
  defaultVerticalPlacement = "bottom",
  ...rest
}: PopoverProps): React.ReactElement {
  return (
    <BasePopover
      name="Popover"
      maxWidth={400}
      defaultVerticalPlacement={defaultVerticalPlacement}
      renderAsSheetOnMobile={renderAsSheetOnMobile}
      {...rest} // eslint-disable-line react/jsx-props-no-spreading
    />
  );
}
```


### ProgressBar

# ProgressBar

A simple progress bar that can be used to display any kind of progress.
If the progress value is bigger than maxValue, it is handled gracefully and the maxValue is set to the progress values. Negative progress value is set to 0.

# Example for basic usage:

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type ProgressBarProps = {
  /** The value for 100% progress. Consumer does not need to do percentage calculation. */
  maxValue: number;
  /** The value already progressed. If progress >= maxValue the bar is filled completely */
  progress: number;
  squareCorners?: boolean;
  weight?: ProgressBarWeight;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
} & HTMLAttributes<HTMLProgressElement>;
```


### PromptInput

# PromptInput

The Prompt Input component is a versatile input field designed for initiating AI-assisted actions such as querying, searching, or suggesting responses.

This component can be used in search interfaces, assistants, or AI-driven experiences that involve freeform text input and follow-up actions. It balances guidance and flexibility by offering placeholder text, contextual slots, and command hints.

## Anatomy

1. **Placeholder Text** (optional):
   Provides guidance on what to enter. Disappears once the user types. Can be customized via the `placeholder` prop.
2. **Right Content Slot** (`rightContent`):
   Optional slot to inject any React node (e.g. icon, spinner, badge). Positioned on the right side inside the input field.
3. **Shortcut Hint** (optional):
   Displays keyboard shortcut (e.g. `⌘+K`) for opening or focusing the input. Aids discoverability and power user efficiency.
4. **Left Content Slot** (`leftContent`):
   Optional slot to inject any React node (e.g. icon, avatar, badge). Positioned on the left side inside the input field.
5. **Submit Button** (optional):
   Button to manually submit the input (allowed icons `search`, `arrow-up` and `arrow-right`). Can be used as an alternative to pressing `Enter`.
6. **Search History Panel** (optional):
   Displays a list of recent queries for quick re-selection. Helps users revisit past inputs.
7. **Disclaimer / Info Area** (optional):
   Displays contextual details or links (e.g. legal info or feature description). Commonly used to offer auxiliary information like “Learn more”.

**[Read more about recommended usage and best practices](#best-practices--when-to-use)**

## Props

## Basic prompt input

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800 }}>
      <PromptInputWithState {...args} />
    </div>
```

## Prompt input with suggestion chips

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800 }}>
      <PromptInputWithState {...args} showSuggestions />
    </div>
```

## Prompt input with autocomplete

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 300 }}>
      <div
        style={{
          width: 800,
          position: "absolute",
          top: 50,
          left: 50,
        }}
      >
        <PromptInputWithAutocomplete {...args} />
      </div>
    </div>
```

## Collapsed variant for placement in nav

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 300 }}>
      <div
        style={{
          width: 800,
          position: "absolute",
          top: 50,
          left: 50,
        }}
      >
        <Stack space="s">
          <div style={{ padding: 30, backgroundColor: "white" }}>
            <PromptInputWithAutocomplete {...args} />
          </div>
          <Button>Focusable button</Button>
        </Stack>
      </div>
    </div>
```

## Behaviors

- **Autocomplete UI**
  Above the input field, contextual suggestions (e.g., Suggestion 1, Suggestion 2, Suggestion 3) may appear based on previous usage or system recommendations.

- **Keyboard Navigation**
  Users can open suggestions with ⌘+K, navigate using Tab or arrow keys, select with Space, and submit with Enter. Improves accessibility and speed.

- **Dynamic States**
  The component supports visual state changes based on focus, hover, typing, or disabled interactions. Useful for showing loading, error, or success feedback.

- **Multi-line Input Growth**
  The input automatically expands vertically when typing multiple lines, growing up to a customizable maximum height. Once the max-height is reached, a scrollbar appears to allow navigation through longer content.

## Best Practices / When to Use

- Use the prompt input when the user needs to interact with an AI system or search interface.
- Maintain clarity in placeholder text and avoid overloading the component with too many interactive elements.
- Submit Button:
  - `search` icon is only used for when the Prompt Input is used for search functionality
  - `arrow-up` icon is used for when the Prompt Input is placed at the bottom of a page e.g.: AI conversation interface like LiSA
  - `arrow-right` icon is used when the Prompt Input is used in a standalone full screen version e.g.: In the dashboard or when used in the top navbar
- Left Content Slot (`leftContent`):
  - Recommendation of components like `SegmentedControl`, `DropdownMenu`, `Link`, `Popover`, `Secondary Button`
- Right Content Slot (`rightContent`):
  - Recommendation of components like `Tertiary button (icon only)`, `PictogramButton`, `DropdownMenu`, `Select`, `Popover`

## Related Components

{/* prettier-ignore */}
- <a href="/?path=/docs/form-searchinput--docs">Search input</a>
- <a href="/?path=/docs/form-textarea--docs">Textarea</a>

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
type StyledEmptyStateTopRightContentContainerProps = Pick<
  PromptInputProps,
  "isNavBarVariant"
>;

const StyledEmptyStateTopRightContentContainer =
  styled.div<StyledEmptyStateTopRightContentContainerProps>(
    ({ theme, isNavBarVariant }) => ({
      position: "absolute",
      top: isNavBarVariant
        ? theme.variables.size.spacing.xxs
        : getPaddingVertical(theme),
      right: getPaddingHorizontal(theme),
      userSelect: "none",
      pointerEvents: "none",
    })

type StyledClearButtonProps = PictogramButtonProps &
  Pick<PromptInputProps, "isNavBarVariant">;

const StyledClearButton = styled(PictogramButton)<StyledClearButtonProps>(
  ({ theme, isNavBarVariant }) => ({
    position: "absolute",
    right: theme.variables.size.spacing.s,
    top: isNavBarVariant ? 0 : theme.variables.size.spacing.s,
  })

// It needs margin due to empty clickable space that focuses the textarea
// Slots should not be in the div, because area between should be clickable also
type SlotProps = {
  side?: "right";
};

type SendButtonProps = Omit<RoundButtonProps, "icon" | "variant"> & {
  icon?: "arrow-up" | "search" | "arrow-right" | "voice";
};

export type PromptInputProps = {
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  header?: BasePromptInputHeaderProps;
  variant?: "ai";
  skipAIAnimation?: boolean;
  bottomToolbar?: BottomToolbarConfig;
  sendButtonProps?: SendButtonProps;
  /** Currently shows send button processing state  */
  isProcessing?: boolean;
  clearButtonAriaLabel?: string;
  hint?: ReactElement;
  /** Shows in the clear button space in empty state */
  emptyStateTopRightContent?: ReactElement;
  onClear: () => void;
  /** Called when user selects (or rejects) a list item from autocomplete list */
  onComplete?: ({
    value,
    category,
    evt,
    index,
  }: {
    value: ListItemValue;
    category?: string;
    evt?: SyntheticEvent;
    index?: number;
  }) => void;
  /** Called on Enter key to submit the form  */
  onEnterKey: () => void;
  /** Renders an icon to the left inside the textarea */
  leftIcon?: IconName;
} & Omit<
```


### SegmentedProgressBar

# SegmentedProgressBar

SegmentedProgressBar can be used to display progress in different segments. For example a progress where you can have correct and incorrect answers to a total number of questions.

The bar **may** have four different segments which have a fixed order: green, yellow, red and gray. Gray represents the not yet finished items.

If the sum of the progress values are bigger than maxValue, it is handled gracefully and the maxValue is set to the sum of the progress values. Negative progress values are set to 0.

## Features

- Displays progress in up to four different segments with distinct colors
- Customizable weight (thin/fat) and corner style
- Responsive to container width
- Handles edge cases like overflow values gracefully
- Supports optional legend popover

## Usage

The SegmentedProgressBar accepts progress values in different variants:

- `success` (green) - Represents positive progress
- `warning` (yellow) - Represents intermediate progress
- `alert` (red) - Represents negative progress
- `empty` (gray) - Represents remaining/unfilled progress

## Props

## Examples

### Basic Usage

```tsx
<label style={{ width: 800 }}>
      <Inline space="s" vAlignItems="center">
        <Text>Test progress:</Text>
        <div style={{ width: 600 }}>
          <SegmentedProgressBar {...rest} aria-label="Progress Bar" />
        </div>
      </Inline>
    </label>
```

### With Legend popover

```tsx
<label style={{ width: 800 }}>
      <Inline space="s" vAlignItems="center">
        <Text>Test progress:</Text>
        <div style={{ width: 600 }}>
          <SegmentedProgressBar {...rest} aria-label="Progress Bar" />
        </div>
      </Inline>
    </label>
```

## Best Practices

1. **Value Handling**:

   - The component automatically handles percentage calculations
   - Negative values are set to 0
   - If the sum of progress values exceeds maxValue, maxValue is adjusted to match the sum

2. **Accessibility**:

   - Use tooltips to provide additional context for each segment
   - Ensure sufficient color contrast for different segments
   - Consider using monochrome mode for color-blind users

3. **Responsive Design**:
   - The progress bar is 100% width by default
   - Adjust the container width to control the progress bar width

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
type StyledSegmentContainerProps = {
  filledPercentage: number;
};
```


### Sheet

# Sheet

A sheet is a UI container that provides an optimized view for mobile devices. It can be used for various types of UI, including popover, modal, or overflow menu, both in modal and non-modal contexts.

## General Properties

- The sheet utilizes the full width of the viewport and remains fixed to the bottom of the screen.
- The height of the sheet is flexible and will adjust to fit its content.
- If the content is long, the sheet will expand up to a maximum height of 80% of the available viewport. This allows the content to be scrollable.
- The sheet can be dismissed by tapping outside of it or swiping it down.

## Example

```tsx
{
    const content = (
      <Box space="m">
        <Stack>
          <H4>Malignant tumors</H4>
          <Text color="tertiary">
            Malignant
            <i>from Latin malignus = “malignant” and tumor = “swelling”</i>
          </Text>
          <Text>
            Malignant tissue neoplasms. In general, malignant tumors are
            characterized by rapid and aggressive growth. They tend to grow in a
            locally destructive and invasive-infiltrating manner, meaning they
            tend to metastasize. Depending on the extent of the primary tumor,
            the presence of lymph node involvement and/or metastases, malignant
            tumors are classified (so-called staging, usually using TNM
            classification). The degree of differentiation of the tumor tissue
            is also taken into account in the assessment (so-called grading).
          </Text>
          <Text>
            Relocations of a malignant tumor, which is then also referred to as
            a primary tumor. Metastases arise from the detachment of tumor
            cells, which reach another region of the body in different ways
            (e.g. with the lymph = &quot;lymphogenic&quot; or with the blood =
            &quot;hematogenous&quot;), remain there and continue to multiply.
            The term metastasis is rarely used in connection with septic
            pathogen spread (e.g. in endocarditis).
          </Text>

          <Inline space="s" vAlignItems="top">
            <Icon color="accent" name="article" />
            <Link href="http://example.com" target="_blank" color="accent">
              General oncology ›  Definition and classification of tumors
            </Link>
          </Inline>
        </Stack>
      </Box>
    );

    return (
      <SheetWithState {...args} showLoading>
        {content}
      </SheetWithState>
    )
```

## With Backdrop on mobile

```tsx
<SheetWithState {...args} hasBackdrop>
      {content}
    </SheetWithState>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type SheetProps = {
  /* Id to associate with a trigger */
  id?: string;
  /** contents */
  children: React.ReactNode;
  /* Option to show backdrop */
  hasBackdrop?: boolean;
  /* Custom portal container to render sheet into */
  portalContainer?: HTMLElement;
  /* used to show / hide sheet */
  isVisible: boolean;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  /* Called when sheet needs to close, on escape, outside click, swipe down, etc. */
  onClose: () => void;
  /* Called when sheet is removed from DOM after exit animation. */
  onUnmount?: () => void;
  /* Controls whether Sheet closes on outside click */
  dismissOnOutsideClick?: boolean;
  /* Option for focus-trap, controls whether the first focuable item recieves focus */
  disableInitialFocus?: boolean;
  /* Option for focus-trap, controls whether the trigger should receive back the focus on popover close */
  disableReturnFocusToTrigger?: boolean;
  /* Callback for whether outside click should deactivate. Needed to handle the case where outside click is on the trigger */
  onClickOutsideDeactivates?: (evt: MouseEvent) => boolean;
  /* Role for accessibility */
  role?: PopupRole;
};

type StyledContainerProps = {
  containerHeight?: number;
  isClosing?: boolean;
  isOpening?: boolean;
  dragOffset?: number;
};

type StyledContentContainerProps = {
  isClosing?: boolean;
  isOpening?: boolean;
};

type StyledHandleContainerProps = {
  isContentContainerScrolled?: boolean;
};
```


### SubThemeProvider

# **SubThemeProvider**

SubThemeProvider is the best way to provide contrast color to your components like notifications or different banners.

All you need is just to wrap your markup with `<SubThemeProvider />` component.

> Note that not all tokens are enabled in sub-themes. Make sure to add missing ones to "tokens/themes/sub-themes/...".

> Components that has "Sub-themed" badge are already in.

### Basic **componentName** usage

> This is very basic usage of **componentName**. Just wrap whatever you have in SubThemeProvider.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800 }}>
      <SubThemeProvider name={name}>
        <Card title={`${name} sub theme is enabled`}>
          <CardBox>
            <Stack>
              <Inline alignItems="spaceBetween" vAlignItems="center">
                <Inline vAlignItems="center">
                  <Icon color="accent" name="bulb" />
                  <Text color="accent">
                    {" "}
                    Example accent text{" "}
                    <Link href="https://amboss.com">this is a link</Link>
                  </Text>
                </Inline>
                <Button variant="primary">Go</Button>
              </Inline>
              <Inline alignItems="spaceBetween" vAlignItems="center">
                <Inline vAlignItems="center">
                  <Icon color="primary" name="bulb" />
                  <Text color="primary">
                    {" "}
                    Example primary text{" "}
                    <Link href="https://amboss.com">this is a link</Link>
                  </Text>
                </Inline>
                <Button variant="secondary">Go</Button>
              </Inline>
              <Inline alignItems="spaceBetween" vAlignItems="center">
                <Inline vAlignItems="center">
                  <Icon color="tertiary" name="bulb" />
                  <Text color="tertiary">
                    {" "}
                    Example tertiary text{" "}
                    <Link href="https://amboss.com">this is a link</Link>
                  </Text>
                </Inline>
                <Button variant="tertiary">Go</Button>
              </Inline>
            </Stack>
          </CardBox>
        </Card>
      </SubThemeProvider>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
type BaseProps = {
  name: SubThemeName;
  as?: "div";
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  children: React.ReactNode[] | React.ReactNode;
};

export type SubThemeProviderProps = BaseProps;

type DeepPartial<T> = {
  [P in keyof T]?: DeepPartial<T[P]>;
};
```


### Tabs

# Tabs

## Props

### Icon Property

The `icon` property in each tab object supports two formats:

- **String format**: `icon: "iconName"` - Icon will be positioned on the left side (default)
- **Object format**: `icon: { name: "iconName", position: "left" | "right" }` - Allows explicit positioning

## Important

The state of this component is controlled outside.
Meaning that if you want to switch the tabs, you should keep a state of it in the parent.

#### Code example

<Source
  language="tsx"
  dark
  code={` // your application
export const YourFancyComponent = () => {
  const [activeTab, setActiveTab] = useState(0)
  const tabs={[
    {
      label: 'tab1',
      icon: 'home' // icon on the left (default)
    },
    {
      label: 'tab2',
      icon: { name: 'external-link', position: 'right' } // icon on the right
    }
  ]}
  return (
  <Card>
    <Tabs
      activeTab={activeTab}
      onTabSelect={(selectedTab) => {
        setActiveTab(selectedTab);
      }}
      tabs={tabs}
      horizontalPadding={["m", "l", "l"]}
    >
      <CardBox>
          <Text>{["content 1", "content 2"][activeTab]}</Text>
      </CardBox>
    </Tabs>
  </Card>
  );
}`}
/>

### Basic Tabs usage

This is a very basic use of Tabs.

```tsx
<TabsWithState {...args} />
```

### Tabs with sublabels

A tab can also contain a sublabel.

```tsx
<TabsWithState {...args} />
```

### Tabs with icons

Each tab header can contain an icon. Icons can be positioned on the left (default) or right side of the label.

```tsx
<TabsWithState {...args} />
```

### Tabs with mixed icon positions

You can use tabs with icons on different position.

```tsx
<TabsWithState {...args} />
```

### Tabs with links

Each tab header can contain a link to another url. Specify `as` prop to fit your needs. For
example in case if you want to have links.

```tsx
<TabsWithState {...args} />
```

### Tabs on a Card

Tabs can be used within a `<Card />` component

```tsx
<TabsWithStateOnCard {...args} />
```

### Scrolling Tabs

A set of multiple tabs will be supported with a horizontal scroll.

```tsx
<TabsWithStateOnCard {...args} />
```


### Tag

# Tag

Tags are used to label, classify, or organize a set of objects using keywords that describe them. Labelling different objects in a set can help users identify and locate specific items more quickly. To indicate a status or count of an object, use <a href="/?path=/docs/components-badge-badge--docs" target="_blank">Badge</a> instead.

## Props

## Variants

### Basic Tag

The default color variant for the tag component is `gray`.

### Blue Tag

In cases where a stronger visual indicator is needed, an alternative blue color variant can be used.

### Removable Tag

Removable tags are used in tasks where users can input multiple criteria, such as in multi-select controls or faceted search flow. They allow users to visualize and manage their inputs as needed.

### Text Overflow

The maximum width of the tag is 200px. Long labels will be truncated.

## Best practices / When to use

- Use tags when content or an item is mapped to multiple categories, and the user needs a way to differentiate between them.
- Avoid using color variants simply for decoration purposes.
- Tags can increase visual noise, especially when combined with other labeling elements. Therefore, use them in moderation.
- Use [Tag group](?path=/docs/components-taggroup--docs) component when multiple tags are displayed together. All of the tags inside of a tag group should be of the same type and color to provide a consistent user experience.

## Content guidelines

- Labels should be short and concise for easy scanning.
- Labels should be written in sentence case.

## Related components

### Tag vs Badge

Tags and badges are visually similar, but they serve different purposes and are appropriate for different user tasks.

<table width="80%">
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th></th>
      <th width="50%">Tag</th>
      <th width="50%">Badge</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>Main purpose</td>
      <td>
        Used to classify, label or organize items with descriptive keywords
      </td>
      <td>Used to indicate status, count or category of content</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Visual appearance</td>
      <td>Compact and unobtrusive, designed to complement the main content</td>
      <td>
        More eye-catching than tag. It uses contrasting color or icons to stand
        out.
      </td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Interaction</td>
      <td>Can be interactive, allowing user to manage their input</td>
      <td>Not interactive</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>Variability</td>
      <td>Remain consistent across interface</td>
      <td>
        Can be varied and can “change states” dynamically to reflect real-time
        updates, i.e. in a status badge
      </td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>


### TagGroup

# Tag group

A tag group controls the layout and behavior for a collection of tags.

## Props

## Best practices / When to use

Tags are often presented as a group, and when horizontal space is limited, the individual tags may wrap to form another line. Consistent horizontal and vertical spacing is important to create a well-balanced layout.

In most cases, all of the tags inside of a tag group should be of the same type and color to provide a consistent user experience.

If there are a large number of tags, it can be helpful to limit the number of tags displayed by default to maintain readability. For example, you could show two lines of tags and indicate the number of remaining tags that are hidden.

## Examples

### Basic tag group

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 600, height: 60, position: "relative" }}>
      <div style={{ position: "absolute" }}>
        <TagGroup {...args} />
      </div>
    </div>
```

### Removable tag group

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 600, height: 60, position: "relative" }}>
      <div style={{ position: "absolute" }}>
        <TagGroupWithState {...args} />
      </div>
    </div>
```

## Related components

- [Tag](?path=/docs/components-tag--docs)

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
type BaseTagGroupProps = {
  tags: TagProps[];
  colorVariant?: TagProps["colorVariant"];
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
};

type ConditionalTagGroupProps =
  | {
      isTagRemovable?: false;
      onRemoveTag?: never;
    }

export type TagGroupProps = BaseTagGroupProps & ConditionalTagGroupProps;

const shrink = (currentWidth: number) =>
  keyframes({
    from: {
      width: `${currentWidth}px`,
    },
    to: {
      width: "0px",
    },
  });

type StyledTagContainerProps = {
  toBeRemoved?: boolean;
  tagWidth?: number;
};
```


### Toast

# Toast

A toast is a lightweight, non-intrusive component that provides brief feedback about the outcome of an action. It automatically disappears after a short duration and should not be used for critical messages or to prompt users for actions requiring immediate attention or input.

## Anatomy

1. **Icon (optional)**
   An icon acts as a supplementary visual element. While helpful for quick recognition, icons can be customized or removed, particularly for longer messages where they might create visual clutter.
2. **Toast message**
   The toast message should be directly relevant to the process being performed and kept concise. For longer messages, which should be exceptions, consider adjusting the display duration to ensure readability.
3. **Action (optional)**
   Toasts can include a single action. This action should not be critical to completing the user's current task.
4. **Close button (x)**
   The close button allows the user to manually dismiss the toast.

## Props

## Usage

Wrap your application within `ToastProvider` to manage toasts within it. ToastProvider ensures that latest toast replaces any previous toasts currently displayed, whether they are auto-dismissable or not.

```javascript

function MyApp({children}) {
  return (
    <ToastProvider>
      {children}
    </ToastProvider
  )
}

<MyApp>
  ...
  <Toast isVisible={condition} message="Some message" onDismiss={handleDismiss} />
</MyApp>

```

## General properties & examples

### **Functional variants**

Toasts are available in four functional color variants. Select the appropriate variant to maintain a cohesive user experience:

- **Success**: Indicates positive feedback, e.g., when a user’s changes are successfully saved.
- **Error**: Indicates negative feedback, used in conjunction with at least one inline error message.
- **Info**: Provides neutral, informational feedback.
- **Neutral**: Used when the message is neutral in tone or when its semantics do not fit in any of the other variants.

### **Dimensions and placement**

- To maintain visibility and readability, toasts have defined minimum and maximum widths. Longer messages are wrapped onto multiple lines to preserve legibility.
- Toasts are positioned at the bottom of the UI, overlaying the main content. In certain cases—such as mobile app interfaces with bottom app bars—adjust the placement to avoid overlapping with other UI elements.

### Basic toast

By default, a toast automatically disappears after 5 seconds—sufficient time for users to read concise messages of up to 5 words. For longer messages or toasts containing actions, consider adjusting the `autoDismissDuration` property to ensure adequate readability.

Only one toast is displayed at a time. When a new toast is triggered, it replaces the existing one immediately, dismissing the previous toast.

```tsx
<ToastProvider>
      <Inline space="m">
        <ToastWithTrigger
          args={{
            ...args,
            type: "success",
            message: "Article saved in 2 collections",
          }}
          buttonLabel="Show success toast"
        />
        <ToastWithTrigger
          args={{ ...args, type: "error", message: "Required fields missing" }}
          buttonLabel="Show error toast"
        />
        <ToastWithTrigger
          args={{ ...args, type: "info", message: "An update is available" }}
          buttonLabel="Show info toast"
        />
        <ToastWithTrigger
          args={{ ...args, type: "neutral", message: "4 users removed" }}
          buttonLabel="Show neutral toast"
        />
      </Inline>
    </ToastProvider>
```

### With action button

Toasts can include a single action, allowing users to respond to the process being performed. However, since action buttons on toasts are not screen reader-accessible, these actions should serve only as added convenience and must not be critical for task completion. For instance, a toast might offer a quick “undo” option, but the same action should also be accessible through other means.

```tsx
<ToastWithTrigger args={args} />
```

## Best practices / When to use

- Use for short messages that confirm an action taken by the user. Ideally, keep the message concise, not exceeding five words.
- Don’t rely on users seeing toasts as they are transient and may be missed.
- Do not use toasts for critical information requiring user action. Opt for a <a href="/?path=/docs/components-notification--docs" target="_blank">Notification</a> or <a href="/?path=/docs/patterns-modal--docs" target="_blank">Modal</a> instead.
- Toasts can include a single action to let users respond to what has just been processed, but the action should not be critical to completing the current task.
- Icons can complement the message but should not convey essential meaning, as they are not displayed on mobile breakpoints.
- For error communication, do not rely solely on toasts. Combine them with the error state of form elements and inline error messages for clarity and accessibility.

<DialogDecisionTable />

## Content guideline

Toast content should be clear, accurate and easy to understand:

- Keep messages concise, ideally no longer than five words.
- Follow the \{noun\} + \{verb\} content formula.
- Avoid articles like "a," "an," or "the" for brevity, unless their omission creates ambiguity.
- Write toast messages in sentence case.
- Exclude punctuation (e.g., periods, exclamation marks, question marks, or commas).

**Good example:** Article saved in 2 collections
**Bad example:** The article was successfully saved in 2 collections

<Notifications />

## Accessibility

- Information in auto-dismissing toasts must also be communicated using another accessible method inline or near the action that triggered the snackbar.
- Actions from toasts are for convenience only and thus hidden from screen readers. These actions must also be made available elsewhere from the page.

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type ToastProps<C extends React.ElementType = "button"> = {
  type?: "info" | "success" | "error" | "neutral";
  iconName?: IconName | null;
  isVisible: boolean;
  message: string | ReactElement;
  autoDismissDuration?: "none" | "s" | "m" | "l";
  closeButtonAriaLabel?: string;
  actionButton?: PolymorphicComponentPropsWithRef<C, ButtonProps>;
  onDismiss: (reason: DismissReason) => void;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
} & Pick<PortalProps, "portalContainer"> &

type StyledContainerProps = {
  isShowing: boolean;
  isHiding: boolean;
};
```


### Toggletip

# Toggletip

A Toggletip is a floating non-modal UI element that opens on demand. It can be used to provide users with additional information, options, or actions for specific elements or tasks, without cluttering the page.

## General properties

- Toggletips can contain text and interactive elements, such as links or buttons.
- Toggletips are displayed when users click on a trigger or when they keyboard-focus on it and hit enter.
- Toggletips remain visible until the trigger is clicked again, there is an outside click, or the Escape key is pressed.
- To ensure that the Toggletip is fully visible, it is position-aware. By default, it uses [auto-placement](?path=/story/components-Toggletip--auto-placements). If there is not enough space on the viewport to display the Toggletip in its default placement, it will automatically use alternative pointer positions and/or placements instead.
- The default placement is on top of the UI element that triggers it.
- The default pointer position is at the center of the Toggletip.
- Toggletips come in two size variants: M (default) and S (compact), with a maximum width of 224 px. If the content is long, it will be wrapped into multiple lines.

## Props

## Usage

```javascript

// Toggletip, use case 1: Wrap trigger element
const App = () => (
  <Toggletip content={<div>Some text with links/buttons</div>}>
    <Link href="#Toggletip">text</Link>
  </Toggletip>
);

// Toggletip, use case 2: Provide external trigger ref
const App = () => (
  <Toggletip
    content={<div>Some text with links/buttons</div>}
    externalTriggerRef={triggerRef}
  />
);
```

## Basic Toggletip with auto placement

```tsx
<ToggletipWithTrigger {...args} />
```

## Toggletip size S

The small size (S) of the Toggletip offers a compact view, making mouse movement between the trigger and the elements inside the Toggletip more efficient. It is a good option to use when minimizing the distance the user needs to travel with a mouse to select an option is desired.

```tsx
<ToggletipWithExternalTrigger {...args} />
```

## Toggletip with specific placement

In certain situations where the default placement of a Toggletip is not desired because it may obscure important information, you have the option to manually specify where the Toggletip should appear.

```tsx
<ToggletipWithTrigger {...args} />
```

## Best Practices / When to use

- Use a Toggletip to present controls or information that doesn’t need to be shown immediately.
- Use Toggletips instead of <a href="/?path=/docs/components-tooltip--docs" target="_blank">tooltips</a> when the content contains interactive elements, such as links or buttons, to make the component accessible.
- It's important to consider the context in which the Toggletip appears. The content of the Toggletip should be relevant to the specific elements on the page. If you need to display lengthy text or a complex user interface, it might be more suitable to use a <a href="/?path=/docs/components-popover--docs" target="_blank">Popover</a> or a <a href="/?path=/docs/patterns-modal--docs" target="_blank">Modal</a> instead.
- Consider an alternative design for touch devices, in which information can be viewed directly without the need for Toggletips or interaction.

<OverlayDecisionTable />

## Accessibility

- The Toggletip is triggered by a click event.
- When the Toggletip is opened, the trigger has the `aria-expanded` attribute set to true, and the `aria-controls` attribute pointing to the Toggletip content.
- When the Toggletip is opened, focus is transferred to the first interactive element within the Toggletip. Focus remains trapped inside the Toggletip until it is dismissed.
- The Toggletip can be closed by clicking the trigger, clicking outside of it, or pressing the Escape key.

## Related components

{/* prettier-ignore */}
- <a href="/?path=/docs/components-tooltip--docs" target="_blank">Tooltip</a>
- <a href="/?path=/docs/patterns-modal--docs" target="_blank">Modal</a>
- <a href="/?path=/docs/components-popover--docs" target="_blank">Popover</a>

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type ToggletipProps = Omit<
  BasePopoverProps,
  | "name"
  | "defaultVerticalPlacement"
  | "hideArrow"
  | "subTheme"
  | "renderAsSheetOnMobile"
> &
  TooltipConditionalProps;

export function Toggletip(props: ToggletipProps): React.ReactElement {
  return (
    <BasePopover
      name="Toggletip"
      subTheme="inverted"
      {...props} // eslint-disable-line react/jsx-props-no-spreading
    />
  );
}
```


### Tooltip

# Tooltip

Tooltip is a brief, floating micro-UI that help users understand the meaning or purpose of UI elements. However, it's important to use this component sparingly. Aim for an interface that is comprehensible without the use of tooltips.

## General properties

- Tooltips can be used to display concise and simple text strings, which is perfect for unlabeled icons or actions.
- Tooltips are triggered when users hover over (and pause on) a UI element or when they keyboard-focus on it. They should remain visible if the user briefly moves the mouse off and back on to the target.
- Tooltips disappear when users move away from the trigger or scroll the page.
- To ensure that the tooltip is fully visible, it is position-aware. By default, it uses [auto-placement](?path=/story/components-tooltip--auto-placements). If there is not enough space on the viewport to display the tooltip in its default placement, it will automatically use alternative pointer positions and/or placements instead.
  - The default placement is on top of the UI element that triggers it.
  - The default pointer position is at the center of the tooltip.

## Props

## Usage

```javascript

// Tooltip, use case 1: Wrap trigger element
const App = () => (
  <Tooltip content="Some helpful text">
    <Link href="#tooltip">text</Link>
  </Tooltip>
);

// Tooltip, use case 2: Provide external trigger ref
const App = () => (
  <Tooltip content="Some helpful text" externalTriggerRef={triggerRef} />
);
```

## Basic tooltip with auto placement

```tsx
<Text>
      This{" "}
      <Tooltip content={content}>
        <Link href="#tooltip">text</Link>
      </Tooltip>{" "}
      has a tooltip.
    </Text>
```

## Tooltip with specific placement

In special occasions where the default placement of a tooltip is not desired because it would cover essential information, you can manually specify where the tooltip should appear

```tsx
<TooltipWithExternalTrigger {...args} />
```

## Toolbar example

```tsx
<Inline>
      <Tooltip content="Save your notes on this question and review them later.">
        <Button size="s" variant="tertiary" leftIcon="edit">
          Notes
        </Button>
      </Tooltip>
      <Tooltip content="Mark difficult questions now to challenge yourself with them later.">
        <Button size="s" variant="tertiary" leftIcon="flag">
          Mark
        </Button>
      </Tooltip>
      <Tooltip content="Add this question to a different session.">
        <Button size="s" variant="tertiary" leftIcon="folder-plus">
          Save
        </Button>
      </Tooltip>
      <Tooltip content="Share this question and session with your peers.">
        <Button size="s" variant="tertiary" leftIcon="share">
          Share
        </Button>
      </Tooltip>
    </Inline>
```

## Best Practices / When to use

- Avoid placing essential information in a tooltip. Tooltips are not highly discoverable and can have usability issues on devices that lack hover interactions.
- Consider an alternative design for touch devices, in which information can be viewed directly without the need for tooltips or interaction.
- Do not put a tooltip on a disabled button. This is inaccessible and unsupported.

<OverlayDecisionTable />

## Accessibility

### Keyboard interaction

- Tooltips appear when the users keyboard-focus on the triggers.
  Focus stays on the triggering element while the tooltip is displayed.
- `Escape` key dismisses the tooltip
- If the tooltip is invoked when the trigger element receives focus, then it is dismissed when it no longer has focus (onBlur). If the tooltip
  is invoked with mouseIn, then it is dismissed with on mouseOut.

### WAI-ARIA Roles, States, and Properties

- The element that serves as the tooltip container has role [tooltip](https://w3c.github.io/aria/#tooltip).
- The element that triggers the tooltip references the tooltip element with [aria-describedby](https://w3c.github.io/aria/#aria-describedby).

### Accessibility issues using 'title' attribute

To prevent double tooltips from displaying, avoid using the HTML title attribute in any children of Tooltip. Moreover, the title attribute is not consistently supported across screen readers and is inaccessible to keyboard-only and mobile phone users. (Referenced from [Atlassian Design System](https://atlassian.design/components/tooltip/examples#accessibility-issues-using-title)).

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type TooltipProps = Omit<
  BaseTooltipProps,
  "content" | "contentPadding" | "maxWidth" | "subTheme"
> & {
  /* Tooltip content */
  content: string;
} & TooltipConditionalProps;
```


## Utilities

### Backdrop

# Backdrop

## Props

The Backdrop component provides a container that can use inverted colors from the current theme while its children continue to use the normal theme.

## When to use

Use the Backdrop component when you want to:

- Apply the inverted theme to a container while keeping child components in their normal theme
- Create dark/light mode sections regardless of the overall application theme

## Examples

### Default Backdrop

A default Backdrop uses the normal theme.

### Inverted Backdrop

When the `inverted` prop is set to `true`, the Backdrop uses the inverted theme, but its children continue to use the normal theme.

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type BackdropProps = React.HTMLAttributes<HTMLDivElement> & {
  children?: React.ReactNode[] | React.ReactNode;
  /** Whether to use inverted colors from the theme */
  inverted?: boolean;
};
```


### ContainerQuery

# ContainerQuery

To use container queries (more on <a href="https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/CSS_containment/Container_queries" target="_blank">MDN</a>), you need to declare a containment context on an element so that the browser knows you might want to query the dimensions of this container later. For this purposes we provide a wrapper `<ContainerQuery>`.

## Breakpoint rules

It accepts an array of up to 3 breakpoint rules:

```
[
  "@container (max-width: 300px)",
  "@container (min-width: 300px) and (max-width: 350px)",
  "@container (min-width: 350px)",
];
```

### Important: Matching Breakpoints and Rules

The number of breakpoints should match the number of rules you want to apply. For example:

If you have 2 breakpoints but 3 rules:

```jsx
<ContainerQuery
  breakpoints={[
    "@container (max-width: 300px)",
    "@container (min-width: 300px)",
  ]}
>
  <Box space={["xs", "xl", "xxl"]} /> {/* The "xxl" rule won't apply */}
</ContainerQuery>
```

It always match the nearest ancestor that has container‐type: size (i.e. the closest “container”). So if you nest two `ContainerQuery` wrappers…

```
<ContainerQuery breakpoints={…}>
  {/* outer “container” */}
  <ContainerQuery breakpoints={…}>
    {/* inner “container” */}
    <MyComponent />
  </ContainerQuery>
</ContainerQuery>
```

…then inside `MyComponent` any @container rules will be driven by the inner `ContainerQuery`— not the outer one.

## Component usage example

Shrink the container in order to see how paddings of Box are changing depends on the size of the parent container.

```tsx
<div style={{ maxWidth: 400, width: "100%" }}>
      <ResizeableContainer>
        <ContainerQuery
          breakpoints={[
            `@container (max-width: 300px)`,
            `@container (min-width: 300px) and (max-width: 350px)`,
            `@container (min-width: 350px)`,
          ]}
        >
          <Box
            vSpace="zero"
            space={{
              cq: ["xxs", "m", "l"],
              mq: ["s"],
            }}
          >
            <div style={{ border: "1px solid red", height: 100 }}>
              <Text>
                I`m <b>&lt;Box /&gt;</b>.

                Shrink my container.
              </Text>
            </div>
          </Box>
        </ContainerQuery>
      </ResizeableContainer>
    </div>
  ),
};

const DynamicCard = () => (
  <Card>
    <CardBox>
      <ContainerQuery
        breakpoints={[
          `@container (max-width: 250px)`,
          `@container (min-width: 350px)`,
        ]}
      >
        <Columns gap="m">
          <Column size={[12, "narrow"]}>
            <div
              style={{
                height: 100,
                background: "lightgray",
                minWidth: 100,
              }}
            />
          </Column>
          <Column size={[12, "fill"]}>
            <Stack space="m">
              <Text>Resize container and card changes its layout</Text>
            </Stack>
          </Column>
        </Columns>
      </ContainerQuery>
    </CardBox>
  </Card>
);
```

## Show / hide elements in Toolbars

```tsx
<div style={{ width: "100%", maxWidth: 400 }}>
      <ResizeableContainer>
        <ContainerQuery
          breakpoints={[
            `@container (max-width: 300px)`,
            `@container (min-width: 300px) and (max-width: 350px)`,
            `@container (min-width: 350px)`,
          ]}
        >
          <Box vSpace="m" space="m">
            <Inline space="xs" alignItems="center">
              <Visibility visible={[false, false, true]}>
                <Inline space="xs" alignItems="center">
                  <Button variant="tertiary" leftIcon="edit" size="s">
                    Notes
                  </Button>
                  <Button variant="tertiary" leftIcon="flag" size="s">
                    Mark
                  </Button>
                  <Button variant="tertiary" leftIcon="folder-plus" size="s">
                    Add
                  </Button>
                  <Button variant="tertiary" leftIcon="share" size="s">
                    Share
                  </Button>
                </Inline>
              </Visibility>
              <Visibility visible={[true, true, false]}>
                <Inline space="xs" alignItems="center">
                  <PictogramButton
                    label="Notes"
                    icon="edit"
                    variant="tertiary"
                    size="s"
                  />
                  <PictogramButton
                    label="Mark"
                    icon="flag"
                    variant="tertiary"
                    size="s"
                  />
                </Inline>
              </Visibility>
              <Visibility visible={[false, true, false]}>
                <Inline space="xs" alignItems="center">
                  <PictogramButton
                    label="Add"
                    icon="folder-plus"
                    variant="tertiary"
                    size="s"
                  />
                  <PictogramButton
                    label="Share"
                    icon="share"
                    variant="tertiary"
                    size="s"
                  />
                </Inline>
              </Visibility>
              <Visibility visible={[true, false, false]}>
                <DropdownMenu
                  menuItems={[
                    {
                      label: "item 1",
                      onSelect: () => {},
                    },
                    {
                      label: "item 2",
                      onSelect: () => {},
                    },
                  ]}
                  size="s"
                  triggerAriaLabel="Menu"
                  iconName="more-horizontal"
                />
              </Visibility>
            </Inline>
          </Box>
        </ContainerQuery>

        <Box space="m">
          <Text color="tertiary" align="center">
            Resize the container to see how the toolbar adapts to different
            sizes
          </Text>
        </Box>
      </ResizeableContainer>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## Responsive card

The responsive card in both the `Content` and `Sidebar` regions adapts its layout based on its parent width.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: "100%", maxWidth: 1200 }}>
      <ResizeableContainer minWidth={750}>
        <Columns gap="l">
          <Column size={[12, 9]}>
            <Stack space="l">
              <H1>Content</H1>

              <Grid
                columnGap="m"
                rowGap="m"
                templateColumns={["1fr", "repeat(2, 1fr)"]}
              >
                {["Card One", "Card Two", "Card Three", "Card Four"].map(
                  (cardName) => (
                    <Grid.Item key={cardName}>
                      <DynamicCard />
                    </Grid.Item>
                  )
                )}
              </Grid>
            </Stack>
          </Column>
          <Column size={[12, 3]}>
            <Stack space="l">
              <H2>Sidebar</H2>
              <DynamicCard />
            </Stack>
          </Column>
        </Columns>
      </ResizeableContainer>
    </div>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type StyledContainerQueryProps = {
  as?: React.ElementType;
  children: React.ReactNode[] | React.ReactNode;
  containerName?: string;
  breakpoints: ContainerQueryBreakpoints;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
};
```


### Expandable

# Expandable

This utility provides a wrapper with css transition for content that can be expanded.

## Expandable with button trigger

```tsx
{
    // eslint-disable-next-line react-hooks/rules-of-hooks
    const [isExpanded, setIsExpanded] = useState(false);
    // eslint-disable-next-line react-hooks/rules-of-hooks
    const id = useId();

    return (
      <div style={{ position: "relative", height: 300, width: 600 }}>
        <div style={{ position: "absolute" }}>
          <Stack space="s">
            <Button
              variant="tertiary"
              onClick={() => setIsExpanded((expanded) => !expanded)}
              rightIcon={isExpanded ? "arrow-up" : "arrow-down"}
              aria-expanded={isExpanded}
              aria-controls={id}
            >
              Details
            </Button>
            <Expandable isExpanded={isExpanded}>
              <Text>{`I think there's an artist hidden in every single one of us. I sincerely wish for you every possible joy life could bring. In painting, you have unlimited power. You have the ability to move mountains. It just happens - whether or not you worried about it or tried to plan it. Put your feelings into it, your heart, it's your world. I think there's an artist hidden in every single one of us. I sincerely wish for you every possible joy life could bring. In painting, you have unlimited power. You have the ability to move mountains. It just happens - whether or not you worried about it or tried to plan it. Put your feelings into it, your heart, it's your world. I think there's an artist hidden in every single one of us. I sincerely wish for you every possible joy life could bring. In painting, you have unlimited power. You have the ability to move mountains. It just happens - whether or not you worried about it or tried to plan it. Put your feelings into it, your heart, it's your world.`}</Text>
            </Expandable>
          </Stack>
        </div>
      </div>
    );
  },
};
```

## Accessibility

To improve accessibility, the `aria-expanded` attribute is used to indicate whether the content is expanded or collapsed. The button that triggers the expansion should have an `aria-expanded` and `aria-controls` attribute. The `aria-controls` should point to the `id` of the content that is being expanded.
Check the code above for an example of how to implement this.

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type ExpandableProps = {
  /** `id` can be referenced by the button trigger's aria-controls attribute to establish accessibility linkage. */
  id?: string;
  children: React.ReactNode;
  isExpanded?: boolean;
  /** Internally overflow is set to hidden when collapsed. Use this prop to restore overflow on expand */
  overflow?: Property.Overflow;
  reduceMotion?: boolean;
};

type StyledContentContainerProps = {
  isExpanded: boolean;
} & Pick<ExpandableProps, "overflow">;
```


### ScreenReaderText

# Screen reader text

From <a href="https://webaim.org/techniques/css/invisiblecontent/#intro" target="_blank">WebAIM</a>

> There are occasional instances where content should be made available to screen reader users but hidden from sighted users. In the vast majority cases, content that is available visually should be available to screen reader users, and vice versa. Verbose cues or instructions that are only read by screen reader users usually do more harm than good. However, there are a few cases where information or meaning is apparent visually but may not be apparent to screen reader users. In these rare cases, it may be appropriate to cause content to be read by a screen reader, but have the content remain invisible to sighted users

For interactive elements (e.g. button), and elements with certain roles (e.g. dialog), `screen reader only` text can be provided with `aria-label`. Note that `aria-label` is not supported in elements with <a href="https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/ARIA/Reference/Attributes/aria-label#associated_roles" target="_blank">these roles</a>. In addition, `aria-label` on static elements is not equally supported in most browser/screen reader combinations.

## Example

Here's an example of a hidden `Skip to main content` link that allows screen reader users to skip nav and navigate to the main content of a page. You can find more examples at <a href="https://webaim.org/techniques/css/invisiblecontent/#examples" target="_blank">WebAIM</a>.

```tsx
<Stack space="m">
      <Text>
        Below is a{" "}
        <Text as="span" weight="bold">
          Skip to main content
        </Text>{" "}
        link that is visually hidden but accessible to screen readers. It
        becomes visible on focus. Press Tab key to view it.
      </Text>
      <ScreenReaderText className="skip">
        <style>
          {`.skip:focus-within {
          position:static;
          width:auto;
          height:auto;
        }`}
        </style>
        <Link href="#screen-reader-text">Skip to main content</Link>
      </ScreenReaderText>
    </Stack>
  ),
};
```


## Content UI

### Content UI

## Content UI

These components are used to display dynamic rich text content such as Articles, Questions, streaming content from AI chat responses, Flowcharts etc.


### ContentBox

# ContentBox

## BasicContentBox

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 944 }}>
      <ContentBox {...props} />
    </div>
```

## ContentBoxes

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 944 }}>
      <Columns gap="m" vAlignItems="stretch">
        <Column size={6}>
          <Stack>
            <ContentHeading level="H2">US Market</ContentHeading>
            <ContentBox
              type="cave"
              title="Caution"
              content={
                <>
                  Avoid lowering{" "}
                  <ContentLink href="www.google.com" target="_blank">
                    MAP
                  </ContentLink>{" "}
                  by more that 25% within the first hour, except in special
                  cases, as this can lead to{" "}
                  <ContentLink href="www.google.com" target="_blank">
                    Hypoperfusion
                  </ContentLink>{" "}
                  and{" "}
                  <ContentLink href="www.google.com" target="_blank">
                    ischemia
                  </ContentLink>{" "}
                  in certain organs (e.g., brain,{" "}
                  <ContentLink href="www.google.com" target="_blank">
                    kidney
                  </ContentLink>
                  ,{" "}
                  <ContentLink href="www.google.com" target="_blank">
                    heart
                  </ContentLink>
                  ).
                </>
              }
            />
            <ContentBox
```
