## Accessible Box Example

```tsx
<div style={{ display: "inline-block", border: `1px solid black` }}>
      <Box {...args}>
        <Text>
          This is the inner content of a box with aria role and attributes
        </Text>
      </Box>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## Wrong usage of Box compare to Stack

```tsx
<div>
      <Text color="error">Bad usage of Box</Text>
      <Box {...rest} vSpace={vSpace}>
        <Text>I need space only for top/bottom</Text>
      </Box>
      <Text>Text without paddings</Text>

      <Stack space={vSpace}>
        <Text color="accent">Good usage with Stack</Text>
        <Text>I need space only for top/bottom</Text>
        <Text>Text without paddings</Text>
      </Stack>
    </div>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type BoxProps = {
  children: React.ReactNode[] | React.ReactNode;
  /** Represents both vertical and horizontal paddings. When used alone, vertical and horizontal paddings will have the same value.
   * Use "vSpace" prop to override vertical padding value in case you want it different.
   * specify [small screen padding, medium screen padding, large screen padding] or one padding for all screen sizes
   * */
  space?: SpaceSizes | MQ<SpaceSizes>;
  /** Represents vertical padding. Used to override the vertical padding from "space" prop.
   * specify [small screen vertical padding, medium screen vertical padding, large screen vertical padding] or one padding for all screen sizes */
  vSpace?: SpaceSizes | MQ<SpaceSizes>;
  /** right padding * */
  rSpace?: SpaceSizes | MQ<SpaceSizes>;
  /** left padding * */
  lSpace?: SpaceSizes | MQ<SpaceSizes>;
  /** top padding * */
  tSpace?: SpaceSizes | MQ<SpaceSizes>;
  /** bottom padding * */
  bSpace?: SpaceSizes | MQ<SpaceSizes>;

  /** Text alignment prop.
   * specify [small screen alignText, medium screen alignText, large screen alignText] or one alignText for all screen sizes */
  alignText?: TextAlignment | MQ<TextAlignment>;

  /** Aria property for assigning an accessible role to an element
   * eg. dialog, tab, tooltip etc. */
  role?: string;

  /** @deprecated Use aria-* props directly instead */
  ariaAttributes?: AriaAttributes;

  id?: string;

  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;

  height?: Property.Height | MQ<Property.Height>;
};
```


### Columns

{/* Keeping the deprecated ArgsTable here because ArgTypes doesn't support multiple components in one doc and subcomponents have not been implemented in the new version: https://github.com/storybookjs/storybook/issues/20782 */}

# Columns

Our Column system is fully responsive. It follows the 12 grid system which means that:

- The maximum amount of columns is 12 in one row.
- One single column takes a space up to 12.
- If the overall columns sizes adds up to more than 12 total, it will be [wrapped](#column--wrapping).
- It's normal for columns to not take whole row.

## `<Columns />` Props

## `<Column />`

- `<Column />` can be used **only** used as **a direct child** of `<Columns />`.

- Consider using **[Inline](?path=/docs/layout-inline-inline--docs)** in case if you need a row based content based content in `<Column />`.

- Consider using **[Stack](?path=/docs/layout-stack-stack--docs)** to add spacing between children inside `<Column />`.

## `<Column />` sizes

Columns size ranges from 1 to 12 and [narrow](#narrow-columns), [auto](#auto-or-fill) or [fill](#auto-or-fill).

- `size` prop in `<Column />` is [responsive](#responsive-columns).
- [Should I use `auto` or `fill`?](#auto-or-fill).

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Columns {...props}>
        {Array.from(Array(12).keys()).map((i) => (
          <Column size={1} key={i}>
            <ColoredColumn index={i + 1} />
          </Column>
        ))}
        <Column size={12}>
          <ColoredColumn>size 12</ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size={5}>
          <ColoredColumn>size 5</ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
      </Columns>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## `<Column />` wrapping

If the overall columns sizes adds up to more than 12 total, it will be wrapped.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Columns gap={gap}>
        <Column size={6}>
          <ColoredColumn>column 1. size 6.</ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size={6}>
          <ColoredColumn>
            column 2. size 6.

            together with column 1 it makes size equal 12, which fits in one
            row.
          </ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size={4}>
          <ColoredColumn>
            column 3. size 4.
            in sum with columns 1 and 2. it makes size more than 12, so it
            wraps.
          </ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size={4}>
          <ColoredColumn />
        </Column>
        <Column size={4}>
          <ColoredColumn />
        </Column>
      </Columns>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## `<Columns />` Gap

`<Columns />` component provides a `gap` prop.
This `gap` represents both horizontal and vertical spacings between the `<Columns />` children.

> `gap` prop in `<Columns />` is [responsive](#responsive-columns).

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Columns {...props}>
        <Column size={6}>
          <ColoredColumn />
        </Column>
        <Column size={6}>
          <ColoredColumn />
        </Column>
        <Column size={6}>
          <ColoredColumn />
        </Column>
        <Column size={6}>
          <ColoredColumn />
        </Column>
      </Columns>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## `<Columns />` alignment

By default, children within `<Columns />` are aligned to the **top left**.

> Use `vAlignItems` alignment prop in `<Columns />` in case of different height of columns.

> `alignItems` and `vAlignItems` props are [responsive](#responsive-columns).

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Columns {...props}>
        <Column size={4}>
          <VeryTallColumn />
        </Column>
        <Column size={4}>
          <ColoredColumn />
        </Column>
      </Columns>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## `<Columns />` alwaysFillSpace

- Use `allwaysFillSpace` when you want last item to take all left space in a row.
- Note that last column is a size of 4, but visually takes more place.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Columns {...props}>
        <Column size={4}>
          <ColoredColumn>size: 4</ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size={4}>
          <ColoredColumn>size: 4</ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size={4}>
          <ColoredColumn>size: 4</ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size={4}>
          <ColoredColumn>size: 4</ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size={4}>
          <ColoredColumn>size: 4</ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
      </Columns>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## `<Columns />` Order

Using order changes the order in which column items are painted, and the order in which they appear visually. The markup stays the same, thus the sequential navigation order of the items does not change. A use case for this is when the logical order of items is separate from the visual order.
In the given example the 1. column will positioned last visually if it's on small screen size.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Columns {...props}>
        <Column size={3} order={["last", "unset", "unset"]}>
          <ColoredColumn>
            1. last order on small, unset on medium, unset on large screens
          </ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size={3}>
          <ColoredColumn>2.</ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size={3} order="first">
          <ColoredColumn>3. first order</ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size={3}>
          <ColoredColumn>4. </ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
      </Columns>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## Responsive Columns

- `gap`, `alignItems` and `vAlignItems` props in `<Columns />` can be a single value or an array of up to 3 values `[value for small screen, value for medium screen, value for large screen]`
- `size` prop in `<Column />` can be a single value or an array of up to 3 values `[value for small screen, value for medium screen, value for large screen]`
- larger breakpoints can be omitted

In given example the `<Columns />` will change :

- `gap` sizes to **s** on **small screen**, **m** on **medium screen** and **l** on **large screen**

- `alignItems` to : **right** on **small screen**, **center** on **medium screen** and **left** on **large screen**

- `vAlignItems` to **bottom** on **small screen** and **center** on **medium and large screen**

Each single `<Column />` will change their sizes to **12** on **small screen**, **6** on **medium screen**, **4** on **large screen**

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Columns
        gap={["s", "m", "l"]}
        alignItems={["right", "center", "left"]}
        vAlignItems={["bottom", "center"]}
      >
        <Column size={[12, 6, 4]}>
          <ColoredColumn>
            size: 12 on small, 6 on medium, 4 on large screens
          </ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size={[12, 6, 4]}>
          <ColoredColumn>
            size: 12 on small, 6 on medium, 4 on large screens
          </ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
      </Columns>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## Nested Columns

A great use case for nested columns is when you need different gap sizes between different elements.
In this example, the parent `<Columns />` has a gap of size `xl`, and the child `<Columns />` has gap of size `s`

- **Do not** place `<Columns />` directly inside parent `<Columns />`, use a single `<Column />` as proxy.
- Note that colums size is relative to columns container. You can observe that columns of same size are different because they are inside different `<Columns />` of different size.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Columns gap="xl">
        <Column size={6}>
          <ColoredColumn>size: 6. Not nested.</ColoredColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size={6}>
          <Columns gap="s">
            <Column size={6}>
              <ColoredColumn>
                size: 6. Nested in Column of size: 6
              </ColoredColumn>
            </Column>
            <Column size={6}>
              <ColoredColumn>
                size: 6. Nested in Column of size: 6
              </ColoredColumn>
            </Column>
            <Column size={6}>
              <ColoredColumn>
                size: 6. Nested in Column of size: 6. They can wrap!
              </ColoredColumn>
            </Column>
          </Columns>
        </Column>
      </Columns>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## Narrow Columns

This example shows a flexible width layout.

A narrow column (`size="narrow"`) will only grow up to its children width while a `size="auto"` column stretches to full width.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Columns {...props}>
        <Column size="narrow">
          <TallColumn>Narrow column</TallColumn>
        </Column>
        <Column size="fill">
          <TallColumn />
        </Column>
      </Columns>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

### Auto or fill

TLDR; You can use several `auto` sized columns and they'll take the same space, but you can use only one `fill` sized column and it'll take all the rest.

Why can't I use just singular `auto` sized instead of `fill`?

Well, you can. But `auto` behaves like a full fledged column, meanin it'll wrap when needed. While `fill` is a will take the rest width don't matter what, as long is left space if more than 1px.

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type ColumnsProps = PropsWithChildren<{
  /** represents the size of both horizontal and vertical gap between children, can be a single string or an array of 3 strings
   * specify [small screen gap size, medium screen gap size, large screen gap size] to apply different gap sizes for different screen sizes
   */
  gap?: SpaceSizes | MQ<SpaceSizes>;

  /** set to true if you want the last element to fill the space
   */
  alwaysFillSpace?: boolean;

  /** specify horizontal alignment
   * specify [small screen alignItems, medium screen alignItems, large screen alignItems] to apply different alignItems for different screen sizes
   */
  alignItems?: HorizontalAlignment | MQ<HorizontalAlignment>;

  /** specify vertical alignment
   * specify [small screen vAlignItems, medium screen vAlignItems, large screen vAlignItems] to apply different vAlignItems for different screen sizes
   */
  vAlignItems?: VerticalAlignment | MQ<VerticalAlignment>;

  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;

  /** @ignore */
  "data-ds-id"?: string;
}> &

export type ColumnProps = PropsWithChildren<{
  /** represents the size of the column, can be a single number or an array of 3 numbers with a value ranging from 1 to 12
   * specify [small screen column size, medium screen column size, large screen column size] to apply different values for different screen sizes
   */
  size?: ColumnSizes | MQ<ColumnSizes>;

  /** specify the visual positioning of the column. [small screen column order, medium screen column order, large screen column order] to apply different order for different screen sizes
   */
  order?: Order | MQ<Order>;

  alignSelf?: ColumnAlignment | MQ<ColumnAlignment>;
  /** display column at given breakpoint */
  show?: boolean | MQ<boolean>;

  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;

  /** @ignore */
  "data-ds-id"?: string;
}> &
```


### Grid

# Grid

Grid can be used to position content and create complex layouts. It is based on <a href="https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/CSS_grid_layout" target="_blank">CSS Grid</a> and a 12-column system with specific column size to create consistent layouts.

## Basic Grid

Based, on 12-column system, the size of each item can be defined by the number of columns it spans. When an item doesn't fit into the same row, it is rendered in the next row.

Use `columnSpan` to layout grid items at different breakpoints.

```tsx
<div style={{ border: "1px solid gray", width: "800px" }}>
      <Grid {...args}>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]}>
          <StyledContent>One</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]}>
          <StyledContent>Two</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 4]}>
          <StyledContent>Three</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 8]}>
          <StyledContent>Four</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
      </Grid>
    </div>
```

## Layout using areas

Use `templateAreas` to layout grid items by area. To create an area that spans multiple rows or columns, repeat the name of that area in the template.
Here's an example using `templateAreas` with custom column track sizes instead of 12 column layout.

```tsx
<div style={{ border: "1px solid gray", width: "800px" }}>
      <Grid {...args}>
        <Grid.Item area="one">
          <StyledContent>One</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item area="two">
          <StyledContent>Two</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item area="three">
          <StyledContent>Three</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item area="four">
          <StyledContent>Four</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
      </Grid>
    </div>
```

## Layout items with varying heights

You can explicitly position grid items and provide a row span to accommodate items of varying heights. This works well when the heights of items are not dynamic.

```tsx
<div style={{ border: "1px solid gray", width: "800px" }}>
      <Grid {...args}>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={12}>
          <StyledContent>One</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 8]} row={["auto", "2"]}>
          <StyledContent style={{ height: 100 }}>Two</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item
          columnSpan={[12, 4]}
          row={["auto", "2 / span 2"]}
          columnStart={["auto", "9"]}
        >
          <StyledContent style={{ height: 140 }}>Three</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 8]} row={["auto", "3 / span 3"]}>
          <StyledContent style={{ height: 160 }}>Four</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item
          columnSpan={[12, 4]}
          row={["auto", "4"]}
          columnStart={["auto", "9"]}
        >
          <StyledContent>Five</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
      </Grid>

    </div>
```

## Nested grid

A grid can be nested within a another grid. However, keep in mind that a nested grid is independent from its parent grid by default, meaning it won't inherit the parent’s track sizing. To align nested grid items with the main grid, make it a <a href="https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/CSS_grid_layout/Subgrid" target="_blank">subgrid</a> by setting `isSubgrid` to true.

```tsx
{
    const { isSubgrid, ...rest } = args;
    const nestedGridLabel = isSubgrid ? "Subgrid" : "Nested grid";

    return (
      <div style={{ border: "1px solid gray", width: "800px" }}>
        <Grid {...rest}>
          <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]}>
            <StyledContent>Main: One</StyledContent>
          </Grid.Item>
          <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]}>
            <Grid {...args}>
              <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]}>
                <StyledContent isNestedGrid>
                  {nestedGridLabel}: One (columnSpan: 6)
                </StyledContent>
              </Grid.Item>
              <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]}>
                <StyledContent isNestedGrid>
                  {nestedGridLabel}: Two (columnSpan: 6)
                </StyledContent>
              </Grid.Item>
              <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]}>
                <StyledContent isNestedGrid>
                  {nestedGridLabel}: Three (columnSpan: 6)
                </StyledContent>
              </Grid.Item>
            </Grid>
          </Grid.Item>
          <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 4]}>
            <StyledContent>Main: Three</StyledContent>
          </Grid.Item>
          <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 8]}>
            <StyledContent>Main: Four</StyledContent>
          </Grid.Item>
        </Grid>
      </div>
    )
```

## Grid item placement and behaviour

### Align and justify items

By default, grid items stretch to fill their designated area. Use `alignItems` and `justifyItems` to adjust vertical and horizontal alignment of items within their tracks or areas.

```tsx
<div style={{ border: "1px solid gray", width: "800px" }}>
      <Grid {...args}>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]}>
          <StyledContent style={{ height: 75 }}>
            Aligned and justified center
          </StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]}>
          <StyledContent style={{ height: 75 }}>
            Aligned and justified center
          </StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
      </Grid>
    </div>
```

### Align individual item

Each grid item can be independently aligned using `alignSelf` and `justifySelf`, allowing you to control its position within its specific track or area.

```tsx
<div style={{ border: "1px solid gray", width: "800px" }}>
      <Grid {...args}>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]} alignSelf="center">
          <StyledContent style={{ height: 75 }}>Aligned center</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]} justifySelf="center">
          <StyledContent style={{ width: 100 }}>Justified center</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
      </Grid>
    </div>
```

### Explicitly placed item

To explicitly place a grid item, specify the desired row and column lines. Be aware that this may result in a visual reordering of grid elements.

```tsx
<div style={{ border: "1px solid gray", width: "800px" }}>
      <Grid {...args}>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]}>
          <StyledContent>One</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[11, 4]} row="2 / span 2" columnStart="2">
          <StyledContent>
            Two, placed at column line 2, row line 2 (span 2)
          </StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 4]}>
          <StyledContent>Three</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
        <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]}>
          <StyledContent>Four</StyledContent>
        </Grid.Item>
      </Grid>
    </div>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type GridProps = {
  /** Define spacing between columns */
  columnGap?: SpaceSizes | MQ<SpaceSizes>;
  /** Define spacing between rows */
  rowGap?: SpaceSizes | MQ<SpaceSizes>;
  /** Define track sizing for explicit grid row tracks, see https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/grid-template-rows */
  templateRows?: Property.GridTemplateRows | MQ<Property.GridTemplateRows>;
  /** Define track sizing for explicit grid column tracks, see https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/grid-template-columns
   * Overrides 12 column grid
   */
  templateColumns?:
    | Property.GridTemplateColumns
    | MQ<Property.GridTemplateColumns>;
  /** Define layout with areas, see https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/grid-template-areas */
  templateAreas?: Property.GridTemplateAreas | MQ<Property.GridTemplateAreas>;
  /** Define row track size for implicit grid rows, see https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/grid-auto-rows */
  autoRows?: Property.GridAutoRows | MQ<Property.GridAutoRows>;
  /** Aligns grid items along the block (column) axis, see https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/align-items */
  alignItems?: Property.AlignItems | MQ<Property.AlignItems>;
  /** Aligns grid items along the inline (row) axis, see https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/justify-items */
  justifyItems?: Property.JustifyItems | MQ<Property.JustifyItems>;
  /** Displays an inline grid */
  inline?: boolean;
  /** If set to true, it will share parent column tracks */
  isSubgrid?: boolean;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  children: ReactElement<GridItemProps>[];
} & HTMLAttributes<HTMLDivElement>;
```


### Inline

# Inline

Inline is a layout component that adds a defined space between its children.
It provides the ability to wrap the elements if there isn't enough space to fit any more in the same row.

## Props

## Inline example

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Inline {...props}>
        {arrayFrom(numOfBoxes).map((each) => (
          <ColoredBox key={each} />
        ))}
      </Inline>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## No wrap Inline example

You can disable the wrapping property of Inline by setting `noWrap` prop to `true`

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Inline {...props}>
        {arrayFrom(numOfBoxes).map((each) => (
          <ColoredBox key={each} />
        ))}
      </Inline>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## Inline with spacing example

You can add spacing between the children using `space` prop.
The example below is an inline with 5 children, with **xl** spacing between them.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Inline {...props}>
        {arrayFrom(numOfBoxes).map((each) => (
          <ColoredBox key={each} />
        ))}
      </Inline>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## Inline with vertical spacing

You can override the vertical spacing between children using `vSpace` prop.
The example below is an inline with 12 children, with **xl** horizontal and **xs** vertical spacing.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Inline {...props}>
        {arrayFrom(numOfBoxes).map((each) => (
          <ColoredBox key={each} />
        ))}
      </Inline>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## Aligned items example

You can specify horizontal alignment using `alignItems` prop.
The example below is an inline with `spaceBetween` aligned items.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Inline {...props}>
        {arrayFrom(numOfBoxes).map((each) => (
          <ColoredBox key={each} />
        ))}
      </Inline>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## Vertical aligned items example

You can specify horizontal alignment using `vAlignItems` prop.
The example below is an inline with `center` vertically aligned items.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Inline {...props}>
        <TallBox />
        {arrayFrom(numOfBoxes).map((each) => (
          <ColoredBox key={each} />
        ))}
      </Inline>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## Responsive Inline Example

To make your inline responsive, all you have to do is pass an array of 3 values `[value for small screen, value for medium screen, value for large screen]`
`space`, `alignItems` and `vAlignItems` props are responsive.

In this example, the Inline has a spacing of size :

- **xs** and bottom right alignment on **small screens**
- **m** and center alignment on **medium screens**
- **xl** and top left alignment on **large screens**.

Therefore :

- `alignItems` value is `["right", "center", "left"]`
- `vAlignItems` value is `["bottom", "center", "top"]`
- `space` value is `["xs", "m", "xl"]`

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Inline {...props}>
        <TallBox />
        {arrayFrom(numOfBoxes).map((each) => (
          <ColoredBox key={each} />
        ))}
      </Inline>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

### Nested Inlines

Inlines can be nested to achieve different spacing and alignment values.

- Each Inner Inline has a blue border, it's there to help make the distinction clearer. Double nested Inlines have a red border.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid gray` }}>
      <Inline space="l" vAlignItems="center">
        <InlineWithBlueBorder space="xs" vAlignItems="center">
          <Text>Inline xs space</Text>
          <NarrowBox />
          <NarrowBox />
        </InlineWithBlueBorder>
        <InlineWithBlueBorder space="m" vAlignItems="center">
          <Text>Inline m space</Text>
          <InlineWithRedBorder space="xxs" vAlignItems="bottom">
            <Text>Inline xxs space</Text>
            <NarrowBox />
            <NarrowBox />
          </InlineWithRedBorder>
          <InlineWithRedBorder space="xxl" vAlignItems="top">
            <Text>Inline xxl space</Text>
            <NarrowBox />
            <NarrowBox />
          </InlineWithRedBorder>
        </InlineWithBlueBorder>
      </Inline>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type InlineProps = {
  id?: string;
  children: React.ReactNode[] | React.ReactElement;
  /** Represents the spacing between the Inline's children.
   * specify [small screen space, medium screen space, large screen space] or one space for all screen sizes
   */
  space?: MQ<SpaceSizes> | SpaceSizes;
  /** Represents the vertical spacing between the Inline's children. It overrides the inherited vertical spacing from `space` if set.
   * specify [small screen space, medium screen space, large screen space] or one space for all screen sizes
   */
  vSpace?: MQ<SpaceSizes> | SpaceSizes;
  /** Represents elements' horizontal alignment
   * specify [small screen alignItems, medium screen alignItems, large screen alignItems] or one alignItems for all screen sizes
   */
  alignItems?: MQ<HorizontalAlignment> | HorizontalAlignment;
  /** Represents elements' vertical alignment
   * specify [small screen vAlignItems, medium screen vAlignItems, large screen vAlignItems] or one vAlignItems for all screen sizes
   */
  vAlignItems?: MQ<VerticalAlignment> | VerticalAlignment;
  /** Set to true to disable wrapping property */
  noWrap?: boolean;
  fullWidth?: boolean;

  /**  render as list */
  as?: "ul" | "ol" | "div";
  /** Role for accesiblity */
  role?: React.AriaRole;

  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  /** @ignore */
  "data-ds-id"?: string;
};
```


### Stack

# Stack

Stack is a layout component provided to make it easy to stack elements and apply a spacing between them.

- **Do not** use [**Box**](?path=/docs/layout-box--docs) to achieve spacing between components. Use **Stack** instead.

## Simple Stack of elements

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 380, width: 102, border: `1px solid black` }}>
      <Stack {...rest}>
        {Array.from(Array(numOfBoxes).keys()).map((b) => (
          <ColoredBox index={b + 1} />
        ))}
      </Stack>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## Responsive Stack of elements

To make your stack responsive, all you have to do is pass an array of 3 values `[value for small screen, value for medium screen, value for large screen]`
`space` prop is responsive.

In this example, the Stack has a spacing of size :

- **xs** on **small screens**
- **m** on **medium screens**
- **xl** on **large screens**

Therefore, `space` prop has a value of `["xs", "m", "xl"]`

```tsx
<div style={{ height: 380, width: 102, border: `1px solid black` }}>
      <Stack {...rest}>
        {Array.from(Array(numOfBoxes).keys()).map((b) => (
          <ColoredBox index={b + 1} />
        ))}
      </Stack>
    </div>
  ),
};
```

## Nested Stacks

Stacks can be nested to achieve different spacing and alignment values.

- Each Inner Stack has a blue border, it's there to help make the distinction clearer. Double nested Stacks have a red border.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: `1px solid black` }}>{children}</div>
  ),
};
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type StackProps = {
  id?: string;
  /** Represents the vertical between the Stack's children
   * specify [small screen space, medium screen space, large screen space] or one space for all screen sizes
   */
  space?: MQ<SpaceSizes> | SpaceSizes;
  /** Represents elements' horizontal alignment
   * specify [small screen alignItems, medium screen alignItems, large screen alignItems] or one alignItems for all screen sizes
   */
  alignItems?: MQ<StackHorizontalAlignment> | StackHorizontalAlignment;

  /**
   * Represents elements' vertical alignment
   * specify [small screen vAlignItems, medium screen vAlignItems, large screen vAlignItems] or one alignItems for all screen sizes
   */
  vAlignItems?: MQ<StackVerticalAlignment> | StackVerticalAlignment;

  /** height */
  height?: Property.Height;
  children: React.ReactNode[] | React.ReactElement;

  /** Render as list */
  as?: "ol" | "ul" | "div";

  fullWidth?: boolean;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  /** @ignore */
  "data-ds-id"?: string;
};

type StackItemProps = {
  /** Represents the vertical between the Stack's children
   * specify [small screen space, medium screen space, large screen space] or one space for all screen sizes
   */
  space?: MQ<SpaceSizes> | SpaceSizes;
};
```


## Patterns

### Patterns

# Patterns

A pattern is usually a combination of components, which serves as a repeatable solution to a common UX problem in a specific situation. It helps ensure that the various combinations of components will produce consistent behaviour across the entire product.

Examples of UI patterns include:

- Navigation patterns, such as top navigation, side navigation, and breadcrumb navigation.
- Feedback patterns, such as notifications, snackbars, and modal dialogs.
- Content browsing patterns, such as filtering and sorting.
- Content display patterns, such as lists, grids, and cards.

By using established patterns consistently, we improve the user experience by providing a familiar and predictable interface. The users can quickly understand how to use our interface without having to learn from scratch.


### ButtonGroup

# ButtonGroup

A button group displays two related actions in a horizontal row, which becomes stacked on the mobile breakpoint, aiding in arrangement and spacing.

## Props

### Basic Button group

The most common usage of the Button group is when a primary call-to-action is paired with a cancel action, typically seen on <a href="/?path=/docs/patterns-modal--docs#2-transactional-modal-dialog" target="_blank">transactional modals</a> or forms.

### With icons

The Button group can also be used to present call-to-actions that have a relationship. Icons can be displayed when they assist users in identifying actions more quickly. For more information on when to use icons in buttons, refer to <a href="/?path=/docs/components-button--docs#button-with-icon" target="_blank">Button usage guideline</a>.

## Related components

To learn more about how to use individual buttons and to follow best practices, please refer to <a href="/?path=/docs/components-button--docs#best-practices--when-to-use" target="_blank">Button usage guideline</a>.

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type ButtonGroupButtonProps = {
  text: string;
  ref?: RefObject<HTMLButtonElement>;
} & ButtonProps;

export type ButtonGroupProps = {
  /** Text and other props for action Button */
  actionButton?: ButtonGroupButtonProps;
  /** Text and other props for cancel Button */
  secondaryButton?: ButtonGroupButtonProps;
  /** @deprecated Use secondaryButton to pass in text and other props for cancel button */
  cancelButtonText?: string;
  onButtonClick?: (action: "cancel" | "action") => void;
  space?: MQ<SpaceSizes> | SpaceSizes;
  /** Action and Cancel button size */
  buttonSize?: ButtonSize;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
};
```


### EmptyState

# EmptyState

The EmptyState component is used to communicate to users when there's no content to display. It provides a clear visual indicator with optional actions to help users resolve the empty state.

## Usage

## Basic

A basic empty state without any actions. Use this when you want to inform users about an empty state without providing immediate actions.

## All Variants

Empty states with different type variants showing the default icons and colors. Choose the appropriate type based on the context:

- **brand**: For positive, brand-aligned empty states
- **warning**: To draw attention to potential issues
- **error**: When something went wrong
- **neutral**: For general informational states

```tsx
<Stack space="l">
      <EmptyState
        title="Brand empty state"
        description="Use this for positive, brand-aligned empty states"
        variant="brand"
      />
      <EmptyState
        title="Warning empty state"
        description="Use this to draw attention to potential issues"
        variant="warning"
      />
      <EmptyState
        title="Error empty state"
        description="Use this when something went wrong"
        variant="error"
      />
      <EmptyState
        title="Neutral empty state"
        description="Use this for general informational states"
        variant="neutral"
      />
    </Stack>
```

## Custom Icon

You can override the default icon for any type with a custom icon.

## With Link

Use `EmptyState.WithLink` when there's a single primary action that navigates to another page.

```tsx
<EmptyState {...args}>
      <EmptyState.Link href="#" iconRight={{ name: "external-link" }}>
        How does it work?
      </EmptyState.Link>
    </EmptyState>
```

## With Buttons

Use `EmptyState.WithButtons` when you need multiple actions or non-navigation actions.

```tsx
<EmptyState {...args}>
      <EmptyState.ButtonGroup
        actionButton={{ text: "Create item" }}
        secondaryButton={{ text: "Learn more" }}
      />
    </EmptyState>
```

```tsx
<EmptyState {...args}>
      <EmptyState.ButtonGroup actionButton={{ text: "Retry" }} />
    </EmptyState>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type EmptyStateProps = {
  /** The main heading text */
  title: string;
  /** The HTML tag to use for the title */
  titleAs?: HeaderProps["as"];
  /** Optional descriptive text below the title */
  description?: string;
  /** Visual style variant that determines default icon and color scheme */
  variant?: "brand" | "warning" | "error" | "neutral";
  /** Custom icon to override the default for the type */
  icon?: IconName;
  /** Optional action element: either EmptyState.ButtonGroup or EmptyState.Link */
  children?:
    | ReactElement<typeof EmptyStateButtonGroup>
    | ReactElement<typeof EmptyStateLink>;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
};
```


### Modal

# Modal

A modal dialog is used when the system needs attention or input from the user to complete a specific task. It informs the user with information that is critical to their current workflow and when the focus of user’s attention is needed. As this pattern is disruptive and blocks the user from their previous context, use it sparingly and keep the content presented short and concise.

## Anatomy

1.  **Header**
    a) **Title (mandatory):** Use clear and concise question or statement that is easy to understand.
    b) **Subtitle or label (optional):** Use one of these optional element to provide a clear context while keeping the title short and concise.
2.  **Body**
    Contains the information and/or inputs needed to complete the workflow that the user is in. There can be a wide range of elements available in the body of a modal dialog, e.g. text, list, forms, other controls, etc. The default component provides a simple paragraph of text, but it is customizable
    and can host any type of UI elements.

3.  **Footer with actions**
    Depending on the type of modal (see common use cases), the system will provide different combinations of call-to-action elements needed to complete the workflow or cancel it. This varies based on the modal variant. If you want to use a custom footer, keep the number of actions and elements minimum for good usability.
4.  **Supporting image (optional)**
    Should only be used when an image or illustration will help convey the message faster. Do not add an image as a merely decorative element. The aspect ratio of the image is 16:9. The image will be hidden in viewports with a height of less than 420px.
5.  **Close button (X)**
    The close button allows the user to dismiss the dialog without submitting any data.
6.  **Backdrop**
    Screen overlay that covers the main page content – allowing the user to focus on the critical task the system asking them to do. The user can also dismiss the modal by clicking on the backdrop. This functions the same way as clicking x.

## Props

> Just define a node `<div class="amboss-ds-portal" />` in your `body` otherwise the modal will append as the last element of your `body` automatically.

## Subcomponents

### Modal.Stack

**Do use** `Modal.Stack` insted of regular `Stack` in order to maintain the special sizes in Modal. It gives us a control and consistansy of Modal's content.

### Modal.Text

A text of Modal has unique style therefore the Modal controls it. **Do use** it instead of regular `Text` component.

## Functional variants

### 1. Acknowledgement modal dialog

Used when an acknowledgement of the information from the user is required. Contains only a single button, commonly "Okay".

When asking users to acknowledge that the action they are taking could have a critical negative effect, such as losing progress that cannot be retrieved, display the destructive action prominently and include additional visual indicators if needed. This ensures that users fully understand the potential consequences before proceeding.

```tsx
<StatefulDestructiveModal {...args} viewMode={viewMode} />
```

### 2. Transactional modal dialog

Requires an action (or several actions) to be taken in order for the modal to be completed and closed. Usually contains a cancel and primary action buttons.

## Size and placement variants

### 1. Size M (default)

The default-sized modal has a fixed width of 560px. The height of the modal is flexible, wrapping the content and utilizing the entire height of the available viewport. The modal is placed at the center of the screen.

### 2. Size L

The large modal (size L) is useful for more complex tasks, such as filtering or settings views. It has a fixed width of 720px. By default, it uses the full height of the available viewport but will not exceed a height of 800px.

However, when needed, there is an option to set the height to wrap the content instead

```tsx
<StatefulModal
      {...args}
      viewMode={viewMode}
      isOpenByDefault={viewMode !== "docs"}
      buttonLabel="Open size L"
    />
```

### 3. Fullscreen

In certain workflows where users need a distraction-free environment to edit content or perform a task, while maintaining a parent-child relationship of accessing the content, a fullscreen modal can be an appropriate choice. The modal will occupy the entire width and height of the screen, while keeping small margins and ensuring the backdrop is still visible.

By default, the content has a max-width of 1280px to maintain good readability. However, there is an option to opt out of this restriction and use the full available space when necessary.

```tsx
<StatefulModal
      {...args}
      viewMode={viewMode}
      isOpenByDefault={viewMode !== "docs"}
      buttonLabel="Open fullscreen modal"
    />
```

## Layout variants

### 1. Default – Left alignment

This is a default layout variant and should be the first layout of choice. It provides good content legibility and scanability, and works well for various combinations of content type needed for transactional modal dialog.

### 2. Mobile layout

To optimise the layout and interaction for touch devices in small size, reduce the padding size and extend the call-to-action to be full width.

### 3. Custom layout

Only customise the layout of the modal when the default component and pattern absolutely does not fit your need. Use the pattern as a foundation and modify the `body` section.

## Best practices / When to use

- Modal dialogs are invasive and should be used sparingly. The ability of the user to access and view their own content is paramount to a good experience.
- Modals retain focus until dismissed or an action has been taken, such as choosing a setting. They shouldn't be obscured by other elements or appear partially on screen.
- Try to limit the number of interactions in a modal. Simplify by removing unnecessary elements or content that does not support the task.
- Avoid multiple steps that require navigation within the modal dialog. Consider using a full page instead.
- Avoid complex decision making that requires additional sources of information unavailable in the modal.
- This modal dialog is responsive. When adding contents, respect the dimensions set by modal dialog to maintain responsiveness.
- Modals should be dismissible and it is best to support multiple methods for dismissal.

<DialogDecisionTable />

### Modal vs Notification

Modals are highly disruptive and block tasks until dismissed by the user. A modal should only be used as a form of notification if it provides the user with critical information immediately related to their task. For non-critical messaging, consider using a toast or notification banner instead of a modal. Toast and inline notifications provide users with non-disruptive feedback or the status of the system without breaking their workflow.

## States

### Loading state

In the case that the loading state is shown after a request and been sent to the server and a response is not instant, disable any interactive elements on the modal (including all dismissing mechanisms) to send a clear signal to the users that no edits or cancellation is possible during the ongoing request.

## Accessibility

- The first interactive element should be auto-focused (it's going to be added)
- Focus should remain inside the modal until it is dismissed
- After the modal is dismissed, the focus should return to the element that initiate the modal
- Allow the usage of `Esc` key to dismiss the dialog

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
type BaseProps = {
  onAction?: (action: "cancel" | "action") => void;
  role?: "dialog" | "alertdialog";
  labelHeader?: string;
  /** The black color is going to be replaced with gray one */
  subHeader?: string;
  /** Text and other props for action Button */
  actionButton?: ButtonGroupButtonProps;
  /** Text and other props for action Button */
  secondaryButton?: ButtonGroupButtonProps;
  /** @deprecated Use secondaryButton to pass in text and other props for secondary button */
  cancelButtonLabel?: string;
  /** Aim to use <Modal.Text> and <Modal.Stack> */
  children?: React.ReactNode;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  privateProps?: {
    skipPortal?: boolean;
    /**
     * @internal
     * Constrains Modal body to available height (applies flex: 1) even with size="m".
     * Prevents Modal itself from scrolling - instead internal content panels scroll.
     * Normally only size="l" gets this layout behavior.
     */
    height?: Property.Height;
    /**
     * @internal
     * Removes all horizontal and vertical padding from modal content area.
     * Use for edge-to-edge content with custom internal spacing.
     */
    zeroPadding?: boolean;
    /**
     * @internal
     * Options passed to focus-trap's tabbable detection.
     * Use `{ displayCheck: "none" }` for testing to avoid JSDOM visibility issues.
     */
    tabbableOptions?: {
      displayCheck?: "full" | "legacy-full" | "non-zero-area" | "none";
    };
  };
  /** It's a bad pattern to use non-dismissible Modal */
  isDismissible?: boolean;
  isFullScreen?: boolean;
  isMaxWidthLimit?: boolean;
  size?: "m" | "l";
  closeButtonAriaLabel?: string;
  /**
   * Determines which element receives focus when the modal opens.
   * - Pass a **RefObject** to focus a specific custom element.
   * - Pass a string that will act as css selector to focus that specific element
   * For and actionButton, secondaryButton, pass a ref to those elements and to this prop
   * For closeButton focus, pass "[data-modal-close-btn]"
   * - If **undefined**, focus falls back to the first focusable element in the modal.
   */
  initialFocus?: RefObject<HTMLElement | SVGElement> | string;
} & Omit<AriaAttributes, "aria-label" | "aria-labelledby">;

/** At least one of header, aria-label, or aria-labelledby must be provided for accessibility */
type LabelProps =
  | { header: string; "aria-label"?: string; "aria-labelledby"?: string }

type ConditionalProps =
  | {
      /** Aspect ratio 16:9 */
      imageUrl?: string;
      imageAlt?: string;
      ImageComponent?: never;
    }

export type ModalProps = BaseProps &
  LabelProps &
  ConditionalProps &
  Pick<PortalProps, "portalContainer">;

const mobileSize = "m";
const desktopSize = "xl";
const getDesktopSpace = (theme: Theme) => theme.variables.size.spacing.l;
const getDesktopInnerSpace = (theme: Theme) =>
  theme.variables.size.spacing[desktopSize];
const getMobileSpace = (theme: Theme) => theme.variables.size.spacing.m;
const getMobileInnerSpace = (theme: Theme) =>
  theme.variables.size.spacing[mobileSize];
// Minimum viewport height on which image is shown.
const MIN_VIEWPORT_HEIGHT = 420;

const StyledModalWrapper = styled.div<Partial<ModalProps>>(
  ({ theme, isFullScreen, size }) => {
    const fadeInModal = useMemo(
      () =>
        keyframes`
      from {
        opacity: ${theme.variables.opacity.hidden};
        transform: translateY(20px);
      }
      to {
          opacity: ${theme.variables.opacity.visible};
        transform: translateY(0);
      }
    `,
      [theme.variables.opacity.hidden, theme.variables.opacity.visible]
    );
    return {
      animation: `${fadeInModal} ${theme.variables.animation.modal.open.duration} ${theme.variables.animation.modal.open.delay} ${theme.variables.animation.modal.open.timingFunction} both`,
      "&:focus": { outline: "none" },
      ...useResponsiveValue({
        width: [
          `calc(100% - ${getMobileSpace(theme)} * 2)`,
          isFullScreen
            ? `calc(100% - ${getDesktopSpace(theme)} * 2)`
            : theme.variables.size.dimension.modal.width[size],
        ],
      }),
    };
  }

type StyledBodyProps = {
  borderBottom: boolean;
  borderTop: boolean;
};
```


### ToolbarControls

# Toggles and controls on toolbars

A toolbar is a group of various controls and actions-such as <a href="/?path=/docs/components-button--docs" target="_blank">buttons</a>, <a href="/?path=/docs/form-segmentedcontrol--docs" target="_blank">segmented controls</a>, or <a href="/?path=/docs/form-select--docs" target="_blank">selects</a>-that allow users to manage or customize how data is viewed. Given the diversity of elements toolbars may host, it’s crucial to maintain cohesiveness and predictable behavior across the product. Use these high-level guidelines to determine the most appropriate UI component for each toolbar function:

## Selecting an Option or View

Use <a href="/?path=/docs/form-segmentedcontrol--docs" target="_blank">segmented controls</a> for closely related choices that adjust a view or setting, enabling users to select one option at a time. On a toolbar, segmented controls function as a radio group, with one option selected by default. Consider the user's context and set the most meaningful option as the default selection.

**Examples:**

- Text editing: alignment selection (left, center, right)
- Article view: content depth selection (high-yield, standard, clinical)

```tsx
<Inline vAlignItems="center">
      <SegmentedControlWithState
        defaultValue="1"
        size="s"
        options={iconOptions}
      />
      <SegmentedControlWithState
        defaultValue="2"
        size="s"
        options={buttonOptions}
      />
    </Inline>
```

## Toggling Options

Use <a href="/?path=/docs/components-button--docs" target="_blank">tertiary buttons</a> or <a href="/?path=/docs/components-pictogrambutton--docs" target="_blank">pictogram buttons</a> with state-indicated labels and icons, allowing users to toggle between two opposing options.

**Examples:**

- Article view: expand & collapse all sections
- Question session view: mark question, show & hide answer stats

```tsx
<ToggleControls />
```

## Good Practice and Exceptions

- **Focus on relevance:** Prioritize the most contextually important controls and actions, ensuring the toolbar remains clean and uncluttered. Move less frequently used functions to an overflow menu when appropriate.
- **Logical grouping:** Visually group related controls and actions to enhance usability and maintain a logical flow.
- **Respect control-specific guidelines:** Refer to the usage guidelines and best practices for each control type, such as <a href="/?path=/docs/form-segmentedcontrol--docs" target="_blank">segmented controls</a>, <a href="/?path=/docs/components-button--docs" target="_blank">buttons</a>, or <a href="/?path=/docs/form-select--docs" target="_blank">selects</a>, to ensure consistency and usability.
- **User experience first:** While these general guidelines serve as a foundation, always prioritize the overall user experience by making thoughtful and intentional adjustments when necessary.

**Example:**

In the Article toolbar, the “Key exam info” toggle is placed beside the content depth selector due to their related functionality. Here, a <a href="#selecting-an-option-or-view" target="_self">segmented control</a> is used instead of <a href="#toggling-options" target="_self">a tertiary button</a> to maintain visual cohesion across the toolbar.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 900 }}>
      <Container squareCorners>
        <Box vSpace="xxl" space="zero">
          <Divider />
          <Box space="xs">
            <Inline vAlignItems="center" alignItems="spaceBetween">
              <Inline space="xs" vAlignItems="center">
                <SegmentedControlWithState
                  options={buttonOptions}
                  defaultValue="2"
                  size="s"
                />
                <SegmentedControlWithState
                  options={[
                    {
                      name: "test",
                      label: "Key exam info off",
                      value: "1",
                      iconName: "key-on",
                    },
                  ]}
                  defaultValue="unset"
                  size="s"
                />
              </Inline>
              <ToggleControls compact />
            </Inline>
          </Box>
          <Divider />
        </Box>
      </Container>
    </div>
```


## Recipes

### CatalogCard

# CatalogCard Recipes

## CatalogCard usage on <a href="https://next.amboss.com/us/courses" target="_blank">CoursesOverview</a>.

### Basic Course Card

In this example, the CatalogCard is used to display the title and optional subtitle of a Course. The title can take up to two lines and the subtitle can use a single line.

### Course in Draft Stage

This is for internal usage only - a status badge indicates the Course is in a draft state.

### Course with Custom Logo

In this example, a custom logo is shown in the top left corner, as is done for NEJMKnowledgePlus courses.

### Archived Course

A status badge indicates that the Course has been archived. We show a dropdown menu on hover, for actions such as unarchiving the course.

## CatalogCard usage on <a href="https://next.amboss.com/us/collections" target="_blank">Collections</a>:

### Basic CollectionItem

The basic collection item, with title and subtitle. A dropdown menu is shown.

### With ColorPicker

The collection item with ColorPicker. A Popover is shown on click.

### Pinned CollectionItem

This CollectionItem has been pinned.

This is a Grid Lasyout containing a row of CatalogCards, they all grow to match the height of the tallest one.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 944 }}>
      <Columns gap="m" vAlignItems="stretch">
        <Column size={4}>
          <CatalogCard
            {...args}
            topLeftElement={
              <Inline>
                <ColorIndicator color={COLORS[0].color} shape="pill" />
              </Inline>
            }
            title="USMLE Step 2 CK"
            subtitle="96 blocks"
          />
        </Column>
        <Column size={4}>
          <CatalogCard
            {...args}
            topLeftElement={
              <Inline>
                <ColorIndicator color={COLORS[0].color} shape="pill" />
              </Inline>
            }
            title="USMLE Step 3"
            subtitle="58 blocks"
          />
        </Column>
        <Column size={4}>
          <CatalogCard
            {...args}
            title="USMLE Step 2 CK"
            subtitle="95 blocks"
            topLeftElement={
              <Inline vAlignItems="center">
                <ColorIndicator color={COLORS[0].color} shape="pill" />
                <Badge text="DRAFT" color="gray" />
              </Inline>
            }
          />
        </Column>
      </Columns>
    </div>
```


### Grid

# Grid Recipes

## Study recommendation list item

Grid usage in <a href="https://next.amboss.com/us/analysis" target="_blank">Session Analysis</a>, Study recommendations list item.
In this example, Grid is used to create a responsive layout for all three rows of the list item using 12 columns. It also contains a nested grid that uses custom column track sizing for content that takes up all free space instead of aligning with the 12 columns.

> Note that this same layout can also be created with <a href="/?path=/docs/layout-columns--docs" target="_blank">Columns</a>, except that the code with Grid is a lot compact and readable compared to nested Columns.

```tsx
<div style={{ width: "944px" }}>
      <Container>
        <Box space={["m", "l"]} vSpace="m">
          <Grid {...args}>
            <Grid.Item columnSpan={[10, 6]}>
              <Inline space="xs">
                <Icon name="article" size="m" />
                <Text>1. Statistical analysis of data</Text>
              </Inline>
            </Grid.Item>
            <Grid.Item columnSpan={2} show={[true, false]} justifySelf="end">
              <PictogramButton
                icon="chevron-up"
                size="s"
                aria-label="Collapse"
              />
            </Grid.Item>
            <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 6]}>
              <Grid templateColumns={["auto", "1fr auto auto"]}>
                <Grid.Item alignSelf="center">
                  <SegmentedProgressBar
                    values={{ success: 0, warning: 0, alert: 10 }}
                    maxValue={100}
                    weight="thin"
                  />
                </Grid.Item>
                <Grid.Item>
                  <Button
                    variant="secondary"
                    leftIcon="quiz"
                    size="s"
                    fullWidth
                  >
                    Qbank
                  </Button>
                </Grid.Item>
                <Grid.Item show={[false, true]}>
                  <PictogramButton
                    icon="chevron-up"
                    size="s"
                    aria-label="Collapse"
                  />
                </Grid.Item>
              </Grid>
            </Grid.Item>
            <Grid.Item columnSpan={[6, 3]}>
              <Box space="zero" tSpace="m">
                <Inline space="xs" vAlignItems="top" noWrap>
                  <StyledKey color="positive" />
                  <Stack space="zero">
                    <Text size="s" weight="bold">
                      0% correctly
                    </Text>
                    <Text color="tertiary" size="s">
                      (0 questions)
                    </Text>
                  </Stack>
                </Inline>
              </Box>
            </Grid.Item>
            <Grid.Item columnSpan={[6, 3]}>
              <Box space="zero" tSpace="m">
                <Inline space="xs" vAlignItems="top" noWrap>
                  <StyledKey color="warning" />
                  <Stack space="zero">
                    <Text size="s" weight="bold">
                      0% correctly using hints
                    </Text>
                    <Text color="tertiary" size="s">
                      (0 questions)
                    </Text>
                  </Stack>
                </Inline>
              </Box>
            </Grid.Item>
            <Grid.Item columnSpan={[6, 3]}>
              <Box space="zero" tSpace={["zero", "m"]}>
                <Inline space="xs" vAlignItems="top" noWrap>
                  <StyledKey color="negative" />
                  <Stack space="zero">
                    <Text size="s" weight="bold">
                      100% incorrectly
                    </Text>
                    <Text color="tertiary" size="s">
                      (1 question)
                    </Text>
                  </Stack>
                </Inline>
              </Box>
            </Grid.Item>
            <Grid.Item columnSpan={[6, 3]}>
              <Box space="zero" tSpace={["zero", "m"]}>
                <Inline space="xs" vAlignItems="top" noWrap>
                  <StyledKey color="neutral" />
                  <Stack space="zero">
                    <Text size="s" weight="bold">
                      Not answered
                    </Text>
                    <Text color="tertiary" size="s">
                      (21 questions)
                    </Text>
                  </Stack>
                </Inline>
              </Box>
            </Grid.Item>
            <Grid.Item columnSpan={[12, 12]}>
              <Divider />
            </Grid.Item>
            <GridItem columnSpan={[12, 6]}>
              <Text weight="bold">1st</Text>
              <H6>Priority</H6>
              <Text color="tertiary" size="s">
                Learning priority based on your performance and exam
                representation. The higher up in the list, the more you should
                study this topic.
              </Text>
            </GridItem>
            <GridItem columnSpan={[6, 3]}>
              <Text weight="bold">4%</Text>
              <H6>Completed</H6>
              <Text color="tertiary" size="s">
                22 questions cover this topic for USMLE Step 1.
              </Text>
            </GridItem>
            <GridItem columnSpan={[6, 3]}>
              <Text weight="bold">High</Text>
              <H6>Representation</H6>
              <Text color="tertiary" size="s">
                This topic represents 0.79% of the practice questions for USMLE
                Step 1.
              </Text>
            </GridItem>
          </Grid>
        </Box>
      </Container>
    </div>
```


### Skeleton

# Skeleton Recipes

## Search result

Skeleton can be used in place of loading spinner when you know beforehand what the content will look like.
For example, here's an example where the page is known to have a container. Thus, when the page loads, a Container skeleton is rendered and then its content skeleton renders before the actual content is loaded.

```tsx
<SearchResultWithState />
```


## Tokens

### Breakpoints

# Breakpoints

Breakpoints define the screen widths at which the layout adapts to provide an optimal viewing experience across different device sizes.

## Mobile-first approach

Our design system follows a **mobile-first** approach, meaning styles are applied by default for mobile devices and progressively enhanced for larger screens. This ensures that the base experience is optimized for smaller devices, and additional styling is layered on as the viewport width increases.

When using breakpoints:
- Base styles apply to all screen sizes (mobile by default)
- Media queries add or override styles at specific breakpoints
- Each breakpoint represents a **minimum width** where styles become active
- Styles cascade upward unless explicitly overridden by a higher breakpoint

## Available breakpoints

<BreakpointsTable />

## How to use in code

### In product using SASS

```css

@use '~style/variables/index' as *;

.container {

  /* This is the default for all devices */
  padding: $sizeSpacingM;

  /* Styles for screens 768px and above (tablets) */
  @media (min-width: $breakpointM) {
    padding: $sizeSpacingL;
  }

  /* Styles for screens 1024px and above (small desktops) */
  @media (min-width: $breakpointL) {
    padding: $sizeSpacingXl;
  }

}

```

### In the design system (React/Emotion)

Within the design system, breakpoints can be accessed through the theme object or by using our responsive helper functions:

```js

// Using media queries directly
const MyComponent = styled.div(({ theme }) => ({
  padding: "1rem",

  // Applies from 768px and above
  [`@media (max-width: ${breakpoints.m.value}px)`]: {
    padding: "2rem",
  },

  // Applies from 1024px and above
  [`@media (max-width: ${breakpoints.m.value}px)`]: {
    padding: "3rem",
  },
}));

// Using responsive helper functions

const MyComponent = () => {
  const padding = useResponsiveValue({
    base: "1rem",     // mobile default
    m: "2rem",        // tablets and above
    l: "3rem",        // desktops and above
  });

  return <div style={{ padding }}>{/* content */}</div>;
};
```


### Colors

# Colors

Colors are not just decorative - they are a means of communication.
Our color system provides a set of functional colors that indicate their
intended usage and ensure sufficient color contrast.

## Roles and Themes

The functional colors are built based on a curated palette of base colors
and are intended for specific usages when applied to an interface.
For example, we apply consistent coloring to components such as text,
buttons, and status indicators. This helps users identify a component
and understand its relationship to its environment.

The system provides two sets of functional colors for the light and dark themes.
When building a new component or UI element, ensure that these colors are used to
meet accessibility standards.

## Tokens

Tokens are a technology-agnostic way to store variables.
We use tokens instead of hard-coded values to ensure better consistency
in UI across different platforms.
These definitions are shared across web and mobile products.

<ColorTokensTable
  categories={[["canvas", "background"], "text", "icon", ["border", "divider"]]}
/>

## How to use it in code?

Click on a element in `Figma` to find out a name of a token you need to use.

<img style={{ maxWidth: "600px" }} src={FigmaTokenImg} />

In our example `text/primary` means that in your code can be used as `theme.values.colors.text.primary.default`

```js
const MyComponent = styled.div(({ theme }) => ({
  color: theme.values.color.text.primary.default,
}));
```

## Applying colors to interface

### Background colors

This set of colors is intended for use on various surfaces of the UI.
To create distinct layers in the interface, use a combination of background
colors and elevations.
Elements that are closer to the user should have lighter colors and higher elevations.

### Text and icon colors

Consistently apply the defined set of text and icon color tokens
to indicate hierarchy, status, and importance.

### Accent colors

The accent color is used as the primary button background,
link color, mobile app bar background, and for certain selected states.
Our accent color for the web product is green, while we use our brand color for the mobile apps.

### Signifying colors

This set of colors can be used to highlight important
information or signify types of messages. For example, the color red
indicates a negative state of error. Use these colors in combination with
other visual cues to ensure that the design is accessible to all.

_\*These colors are not currently available or used in the mobile app's user interface._

## Colors for interactive states

There is a difference in how we handle changing colors for the different states of interactive UI elements on the web and mobile apps.

#### On the web: Dedicated colors for states

For the web interface, we have defined dedicated colors for each interactive element
and its states. For instance, `color.background.accent` is used in Button primary,
and hence, this color will come with state variants such as hover, active, and disabled.

#### On mobile apps: Reusable colors for state layers

When implementing interactive states, we follow the
principles outlined in the [Material Design guidelines](https://m3.material.io/foundations/interaction/states/state-layers).
To achieve color changes in different states, we overlay an additional
transparent color layer over the component's background color.

![](https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/iTOnDTFkxnPSHW_IdlCmbVssCHpXTYfCqDeC-mJmnxiG7URuayxmAGnvoGq5MGXlSu6wYwognBESOp4EmH5Y14QP98Iu0viYcLI46d4_cF4=s0)
See the original image on [material.io](https://m3.material.io/foundations/interaction/states/state-layers#ec68aa40-c1aa-410a-b677-e83f6f2ba021)

In general, we apply a darker transparent layer to indicate the state of being
pressed down, in both light and dark modes. The only exception is for interactive
UI elements with a transparent background in dark mode. For these elements,
we use a lighter transparent color to make the changing states visible against the dark background.


### Icons

# Icons

1. Adding an Icon

   - Create an `.svg` file in the `assets/icons` directory.
   - Specify the icon in `packages/design-tokens/src/web/assets.json` under the `icon` property.

2. Adding a 16x16px Icon

   - Create an `.svg` file in the `assets/icons` directory and add `16` before the file extension. For example: `plus16.svg`.
   - Specify the icon in `packages/design-tokens/src/web/assets.json` under the `icon16` property, ensuring each reference points to the correct file.


### Opacity

# Opacity
Opacity is used to show (or hide) different states of an element.

## Tokens

Tokens are a technology-agnostic way to store variables.
We use tokens instead of hard-coded values to ensure better consistency
in UI across different platforms.
These definitions are shared across web and mobile products.

<OpacityTokensTable />

## How to use it in code?

Click on a element in `Figma` to find out a name of a token you need to use.

<img style={{ maxWidth: "900px" }} src={FigmaTokenImg} />

In our example `30%` means that in your code can be used as `theme.variables.opacity.disabled`

```js
const MyComponent = styled.div(({ theme }) => ({
  opacity: theme.variables.opacity.disabled,
}));
```


### SCSS Theming

## SCSS Theming

Include the theming mixin once at the root of your stylesheet to set up light and dark theme support. Color tokens from the [variables module](?path=/docs/tokens-scss-variables--docs) will then update when the theme changes.

### Setup

```scss
@use '@amboss/design-system/scss/theming' as ds;

// Include at the root of your stylesheet
@include ds.theming($dark-theme-selector: ".dark-theme", $subthemes: all);
```

After that, themed variables in your components switch values when the active theme changes.

### Integration with variables

You'll usually import both modules together. Theming provides the custom property definitions, and variables gives you the SCSS names that reference them.

```scss
@use '@amboss/design-system/scss/theming' as ds;
@use '@amboss/design-system/scss/variables' as *;

@include ds.theming();

.card {
  background-color: $colorBackgroundPrimary;
  color: $colorTextPrimary;
  border: 1px solid $colorBorderSecondary;
  border-radius: $sizeBorderRadiusS;
  padding: $sizeSpacingM;
}
```

### Parameters

The `theming` mixin accepts two optional arguments:

- `$dark-theme-selector` (default: `".dark-theme"`) — the CSS selector on an ancestor element (usually `<html>`) that activates the dark theme. If your app uses a different class, e.g. `.isDarkmodeActive`, pass that instead.
- `$subthemes` (default: `()`) — which subthemes to generate. Pass a list of names or `all` for every available subtheme.

The mixin outputs light theme custom properties on `:root` and dark theme overrides scoped to your dark-theme selector. For each enabled subtheme, it adds a `[data-subtheme="name"]` selector with light and dark color overrides.

### Subthemes

Subthemes override color tokens for a region of the page. Add `data-subtheme="brand"` to a container, and its children pick up brand-tinted colors — backgrounds, text, and borders shift to match. Each subtheme has light and dark variants, so it follows the active base theme. Subthemes only affect color tokens, not sizes or typography.

For the full list of available subthemes and live previews, see the [SubThemeProvider](?path=/docs/components-subthemeprovider--docs) docs.

### Minimal example

If your app only uses one subtheme — say, `brand` for a promotional banner — pass just that name instead of generating CSS for every subtheme.

```scss
@use '@amboss/design-system/scss/theming' as ds;
@use '@amboss/design-system/scss/variables' as *;

// Generate base themes + brand subtheme only
@include ds.theming($subthemes: ("brand"));

.my-component {
  background-color: $colorBackgroundPrimary;
  color: $colorTextPrimary;
}
```


### SCSS Variables

# SCSS Variables

The design system exposes its tokens as SCSS variables. Import them and use them directly in your styles.

```scss
@use '@amboss/design-system/scss/variables' as *;
```

Variables come in two types:

- **Themed** — resolve to CSS custom properties (e.g. `var(--color-background-accent)`). These update automatically when the theme switches, as long as you include the [theming mixin](?path=/docs/tokens-scss-theming--docs).
- **Static** — fixed values (e.g. `16px`, `400`). These stay the same regardless of theme.

## Colors

Color variables are themed — they resolve to CSS custom properties that update when the theme changes. The swatch shows the resolved color for the active theme.

<ScssColorVariables />

## Sizes & Spacing

Static values for dimensions, border radius, and spacing.

<ScssSizesVariables />

## Motion

Durations, easing functions, and transition distances for animations.

<ScssMotionVariables />

## Typography

Font sizes, line heights, letter spacing, font families, styles, and weights.

<ScssTypographyVariables />

## Icons & Utilities

Icon sizing tokens and z-index layers.

<ScssIconsUtilitiesVariables />

## Elevations & Shadows

Elevation and shadow tokens. These are themed and resolve to CSS custom properties.

<ScssElevationVariables />

## Breakpoints

Responsive breakpoint values used by the media-query mixins.

<ScssBreakpointVariables />

## Opacity

Opacity scale values. The preview shows the visual effect at each level.

<ScssOpacityVariables />


### Sizes

# Spaces

<SpaceSizesGrid />


## Typography

### Header

# Header

{/* It would be great to add examples of UI where it supposed to be used */}
We provide different Headers ranging from **H1** to **H6**.

## Props

```tsx
<Stack>
      <H1 {...args}>{children}</H1>
      <H2 {...args}>{children}</H2>
      <H3 {...args}>{children}</H3>
      <H4 {...args}>{children}</H4>
      <H5 {...args}>{children}</H5>
      <H6 {...args}>{children}</H6>
    </Stack>
```

## Header Hierarchy

To create a visual hierarchy on the user interface, use a combination of header variants and colors. For example, use H1 in the primary color and H3 in the tertiary color, as shown below.

```tsx
<Stack space="xxs">
      <H1>Step 1 Prep Condensed</H1>
      <H3 as="h2" color="tertiary">
        30 Topics in 30 Days
      </H3>
    </Stack>
```

## **Custom Header**

This is a flexible header component, where style of text is separated from its semantic tag. It makes it easier to follow the design spec, while keeping the correct internal structure of a page.

### Custom Header usage

- `<H6 as="h2" />` will create a H2 header, which will have styles of H6, according to the design language spec.

```tsx
<H6 as={as} {...rest}>
      {children}
    </H6>
```

## **Header with Long text**

```tsx
<H4 {...args}>{children}</H4>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type HeaderProps = {
  as?: ElementType<any, "h1" | "h2" | "h3" | "h4" | "h5" | "h6" | "div" | "p">;
  children: React.ReactNode;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  /** @ignore */
  "data-ds-id"?: string;
  id?: string;
  color?: BaseColors;
  overflowWrap?: OverflowWrap;
  hyphens?: Hyphens;
  align?: TextAlignment;
} & React.HTMLAttributes<HTMLHeadingElement>;
```


### StyledText

# StyledText

StyledText is used as a formatted text that supports a _limited_ set of HTML elements for text styling.

- **Do not** use this component to parse whole HTML markup.
- **Do not** pass any other type of `children` than `string`.
- **Do** nest this component into a Text component or similiar.

## Supported html tags

- \<b/>
- \<i />
- \<p />
- \<small />
- \<sup />
- \<sub />

## StyledText example usage

In this examples, all supported tags are displayed.

### Simple text styling

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800 }}>
      <Text as="span">
        <StyledText {...props} />
      </Text>
    </div>
```

### Paragraphs

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800 }}>
      <Text as="span">
        <StyledText {...props} />
      </Text>
    </div>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type FormattedElementProps = {
  key: string;
  children: string | React.ReactNode[];
};

export type StyledTextProps = {
  /* The stringified markup to be rendered */
  children: string;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
};
```


### Text

# Text

### Text as Paragraph

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: "1px solid black" }} lang="en">
      <Text {...props} />
    </div>
```

### Long Text as Paragraph

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: "1px solid black" }} lang="en">
      <Text {...props} />
    </div>
```

### Text as span

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: "1px solid black" }} lang="en">
      <Text {...props} />
    </div>
```

### Small Text

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: "1px solid black" }} lang="en">
      <Text {...props} />
    </div>
```

### Bold Text

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: "1px solid black" }} lang="en">
      <Text {...props} />
    </div>
```

### Uppercase Text

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: "1px solid black" }} lang="en">
      <Text {...props} />
    </div>
```

### Secondary Text

> Text with secondary color

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: "1px solid black" }} lang="en">
      <Text {...props} />
    </div>
```

### Tertiary Text

> Text with tertiary color

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: "1px solid black" }} lang="en">
      <Text {...props} />
    </div>
```

> Text with accent color

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: "1px solid black" }} lang="en">
      <Text {...props} />
    </div>
```

### Error Text

> Text with red color

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: "1px solid black" }} lang="en">
      <Text {...props} />
    </div>
```

### Warning Text

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: "1px solid black" }} lang="en">
      <Text {...props} />
    </div>
```

### Info Text

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: "1px solid black" }} lang="en">
      <Text {...props} />
    </div>
```

### Hyphen Text

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800, border: "1px solid black" }} lang="en">
      <Text {...props} />
    </div>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type BaseTextProps = {
  children: ReactNode;
  size?: TextSize;
  weight?: TextWeight;
  transform?: "none" | "capitalize" | "uppercase" | "lowercase";
  as?: "p" | "span";
  /** @deprecated variant property is deprecated now. Please use color prop. */
  variant?: TextVariations;
  color?: TextColors;
  align?: TextAlignment;
  overflowWrap?: OverflowWrap;
  hyphens?: Hyphens | MQ<Hyphens>;
  userSelect?: Property.UserSelect;
  "data-e2e-test-id"?: string;
  /** @ignore */
  "data-ds-id"?: string;
};

export type TextProps = ComponentProps<typeof StyledText>;

export const Text = forwardRef(
  (
    {
      size = "m",
      weight = "normal",
      transform = "none",
      as = "p",
      color: colorProp = "primary",
      align = "left",
      hyphens = "none",
      overflowWrap = "normal",
      variant,
      userSelect,
      "data-e2e-test-id": dataE2eTestId,
      children,
      ...rest
    }: TextProps,
```


### TextClamped

# TextClamped

It's based on **[Text](?path=/docs/typography-text--docs)** component.

## Props

## Basic usage

```tsx
<div style={{ width: 800 }}>
      <TextClamped {...props} />
    </div>
```

**Type Definitions:**

```typescript
export type TextClampedProps = TextProps & {
  /** The maximum number of lines. Default is 1. */
  lines?: number;
};
```
